deaRegistrationStatus.headline: DEA Registration Status — Oklahoma Operators

tracks.status: 60-Day Expedited Window CLOSED June 29 — Dispensary portal closed June 22; effective final day was June 29 per Saturday federal rule. Operators who filed before portal close are in DEA six-month review with safe-harbor. Operators who did not file missed the expedited window — registration at deadiversion.usdoj.gov is still available outside the window (no safe-harbor guarantee). Optional per OMMA; OBNDD mandate applies only to manufacturers/distributors, not dispensaries. Broader-rescheduling ALJ hearing OPENED June 29, 9:00 a.m. ET, Arlington VA — no stay or injunction granted.
tracks.detail: The DEA Medical Marijuana Dispensary Registration Portal opened April 29, 2026 at mmapplication.diversion.dea.gov. Fee $794/yr (PayPal only). OMMA states registration is OPTIONAL for dispensaries — non-registrants may continue operating under Oklahoma state law. JUNE 22 — PORTAL CONFIRMED CLOSED: The dedicated dispensary registration portal (mmapplication.diversion.dea.gov) closed June 22, 2026. Law firm Fennemore Craig confirmed the portal was 'Open Through June 22, 2026' and Foley Hoag LLP confirmed a 'hard stop to registrations on June 22, 2026.' 60-DAY EXPEDITED WINDOW NOW CLOSED (JUNE 29): The effective final day of the expedited window was June 29, 2026 — the Saturday-rule shift of the 60th calendar day (June 27). Dispensaries that filed before the June 22 portal close are in DEA's six-month review queue with safe-harbor operating authority. Dispensaries that did not file by June 29 may still apply at deadiversion.usdoj.gov outside the expedited window but without safe-harbor guarantee. OMMA registration remains optional; OBNDD's mandatory directive applies only to manufacturers and distributors, not dispensaries. Vertically integrated operators also holding a manufacturer/distributor license remain subject to the OBNDD requirement below.

tracks.status: 60-Day Expedited Window CLOSED June 29 — Form 225 with codes 7362/7353/7386 was the only available pathway (dedicated manufacturer/cultivator/lab/distributor portal never launched before the window closed). Operators who filed by June 29 hold safe-harbor operating authority during DEA's six-month review. Operators who did not file missed the expedited window — may still apply at deadiversion.usdoj.gov outside the window but WITHOUT safe-harbor. OBNDD enforcement grace period runs to January 1, 2027. D.C. Circuit: Stay motion FULLY BRIEFED as of July 17 — NDASA and MMJ International Holdings filed petitioners' reply brief; no stay ruling issued as of July 22; court can rule at any time. ALJ broader-rescheduling hearing CONCLUDED July 15, 2026; post-hearing briefs and transcript corrections due August 17, 2026.
tracks.detail: The dedicated DEA application forms for Medical Marijuana Manufacturer, Bulk Manufacturer (cultivator/grower), Analytical Lab, and Distributor did NOT launch before the 60-day window closed. 60-DAY WINDOW NOW CLOSED (JUNE 29): The effective final day was June 29, 2026 — the Saturday-rule shift of the 60th calendar day (June 27), confirmed by Manatt, Phelps & Phillips. This was NOT a formal DEA extension; it was the standard federal procedural rule. Operators who filed Form 225 with medical-marijuana drug codes 7362/7353/7386 by June 29 hold safe-harbor operating authority during DEA's six-month review. Operators who did not file missed the expedited window — may still apply at deadiversion.usdoj.gov outside the window but without safe-harbor protection during review. DEA Form 225 with medical-marijuana drug codes 7362 / 7353 / 7386 is the ONLY viable pathway. Note that June 29 is also the first day of the broader-rescheduling ALJ hearing — file early Monday morning via deadiversion.usdoj.gov and retain timestamped confirmation. Fees under Form 225: $3,699/yr (Manufacturer), $1,850/yr (Distributor), $296/yr (Analytical Lab). DEA has confirmed that operators who apply via Form 225 before the dedicated portal launches do NOT need to reapply. OBNDD (May 8, 2026) states manufacturers and distributors are REQUIRED to obtain DEA registration; enforcement grace period runs to January 1, 2027.

keyDeadlines.label: Oklahoma New-License Moratorium Expiration (Extended to Aug 2028)
keyDeadlines.description: Oklahoma HB 3143 was signed into law by Governor Stitt on May 7, 2026, extending the new-license moratorium from August 1, 2026 to August 1, 2028. No new cultivation, processing, dispensary, or transport licenses will be issued until that date. Companion HB 3144 (2,500-license cultivation cap) passed the House; its enactment status is pending confirmation.

keyDeadlines.label: Federal Hemp Redefinition Takes Effect
keyDeadlines.description: UPDATE (July 20, 2026): Senate Farm Bill markup did NOT open the week of July 21, 2026. Senator McConnell's hospitalization has created an effective 11-11 tied vote in the Senate Agriculture Committee — no markup can proceed without McConnell's return or a bipartisan agreement on a substitute. No new markup date has been set. The November 12, 2026 hemp redefinition effective date is now near-certain to take effect on schedule. Plan for 0.3% total-THC compliance now; do not rely on a legislative fix that has not been enacted. UPDATE (July 19, 2026): Senate Agriculture Committee Farm Bill markup has NOT begun as of July 19, 2026. Committee Chair Boozman conditionally targeted the week of July 21 to open markup, contingent on Senator McConnell's return from hospitalization. No markup has been opened as of this date. The Senate draft Farm Bill does not include a hemp ban delay provision. Hemp redefinition November 12, 2026 effective date is unchanged — plan for 0.3% total-THC compliance; do not rely on a legislative delay that has not been enacted. UPDATE (July 13, 2026): Senate Agriculture Committee Farm Bill markup DELAYED — chair Senator John Boozman (R-AR) stated markup is 'week of July 20 at earliest, probably week after that' following Senator Lindsey Graham's death (July 12, 2026, aortic dissection, age 71) and Senator McConnell's hospitalization; Senate draft Farm Bill reportedly does NOT include a hemp ban delay provision; bipartisan senators reportedly agreed in appropriations discussions to a one-year hemp ban delay (Marijuana Moment) — NOT ENACTED, no legal effect until passed by both chambers and signed. November 12, 2026 effective date remains unchanged — plan for 0.3% total-THC compliance, do not rely on a delay that has not been enacted. Federal hemp redefinition takes effect: 0.3% total-THC threshold (includes THCA); final-product cap 0.4 mg THC; synthetics excluded. Delta-8 / Delta-10 / THCA-flower market effectively eliminated unless reformulated. UPDATE (June 24, 2026): White House OMB Director Vought formally asked House Speaker Johnson to revise the hemp definition or delay the effective date. The House Farm Bill (passed April 30, 2026) did NOT include a delay. Standalone Hemp Planting Predictability Act (S.3686/H.R.7024) would extend to Nov 12, 2028 — bipartisan sponsors but no committee hearing as of July 2026. Plan for November 12 compliance; Senate Agriculture Committee markup window July 13 – ~August 7, 2026 is the last viable 2026 legislative vehicle for a hemp ban delay — monitor Senate Farm Bill markup; Senate draft reportedly does not include a hemp ban delay. Standalone Hemp Planting Predictability Act (S.3686) dormant; no committee hearing as of July 2026.

keyDeadlines.label: OBNDD Enforcement Grace Period Ends
keyDeadlines.description: OBNDD enforcement grace period ends. Oklahoma operators without DEA registration by this date face active OBNDD administrative sanctions, regardless of whether they applied before the June 27 window.

keyDeadlines.label: OBNDD Annual Registration Renewal Deadline
keyDeadlines.description: Annual OBNDD registration renewal deadline for Oklahoma cannabis operators. Failure to renew by October 31 (effective November 1) results in loss of Metrc seed-to-sale tracking access and required cessation of operations — independent of any DEA registration issue. This deadline is separate from and earlier than the OBNDD enforcement grace period for DEA registration (January 1, 2027). Oklahoma cultivators, processors, distributors, and dispensaries should submit renewal applications well before this date.

keyDeadlines.label: Oklahoma HB 2837 — All OMMA Employee Credential Annual Training Required
keyDeadlines.description: Effective January 1, 2027 (HB 2837, signed May 27, 2025), all employees of OMMA-licensed businesses — cultivators, processors, dispensaries, laboratories, distributors — must annually complete an OMMA-approved educational training course and submit proof of completion in their OMMA employee credential application. A fingerprint-based background check through an OMMA-contracted third-party vendor is also required. Training covers state statutes/rules, patient privacy, and safe handling/storage of medical marijuana. Preliminary cost estimates: $250–$500 per employee per year. OMMA is still promulgating implementing rules; businesses should identify approved training programs now and budget for compliance.

keyDeadlines.label: Oklahoma HB 4454 — Edibles Restrictions Take Effect
keyDeadlines.description: Oklahoma HB 4454 (signed Gov. Stitt May 7, 2026) takes effect November 1, 2026. Key restrictions: THC limited to 10mg per serving, 100mg per package (20mg per drink container); bans packaging shapes resembling humans, animals, cartoons, or commercially available candy; bans marketing that appeals to minors. Dispensaries and processors producing or selling edibles must reformulate or rebrand non-compliant products before this date.

items.headline: Bipartisan Lawful Hemp Protection Act Introduced July 22 — White House Backs Bill to Replace November Hemp Ban with Regulatory Framework
items.summary: Representatives Andy Barr (R-KY) and Angie Craig (D-MN) introduced the bipartisan Lawful Hemp Protection Act on July 22, 2026. White House press secretary confirmed support on behalf of the administration. The 60-page bill would replace the November 12, 2026 federal hemp THC product ban with a comprehensive regulatory framework allowing hemp-derived consumer products with up to 1% delta-9 THC (measured on the finished product, not raw plant material), subject to a 21+ minimum age requirement, manufacturing and testing requirements, labeling rules, and restrictions on synthetic cannabinoids. The White House had previously requested Congress delay or revise the November ban; this bill is the legislative vehicle introduced in the House.
items.whyItMatters: Oklahoma dispensaries selling hemp-derived products (delta-8, THCA beverages, hemp flower, etc.) face a near-total ban under current law on November 12, 2026. If the Lawful Hemp Protection Act passes, those markets would be preserved under a regulated federal framework. CRITICAL: The bill has NOT passed Congress — the November 12 deadline remains legally in effect. Senate action is uncertain given the 11-11 Agriculture Committee split (McConnell hospitalized). Oklahoma cultivators sourcing hemp genetics should also note that the November ban covers seeds producing plants above 0.3% THC; the Barr/Craig bill would address this if enacted. Do not assume the ban is delayed — monitor Senate floor action.

source.name: Rep. Andy Barr Press Release — Lawful Hemp Protection Act (July 22, 2026)

secondarySource.name: The Marijuana Herald — Bipartisan Federal Bill Would Preserve Hemp-Derived THC Products Under New Regulatory Framework

items.headline: November 12 Hemp Ban Would Also Criminalize Interstate Cannabis Seed Shipments — Oklahoma Cultivators at Risk
items.summary: A MJBizDaily analysis (July 2026) highlights a less-discussed impact of the November 12 federal hemp redefinition: seeds for cannabis cultivars that produce plants with greater than 0.3% THC will become illegal to ship across state lines. This effectively criminalizes most cannabis seed banks and interstate genetics commerce. Seed prices are already projected to rise 50-200% post-deadline. Some operators are exploring in-state clones and tissue culture as alternatives with a potentially different risk profile under the new statutory language.
items.whyItMatters: Oklahoma cannabis cultivators who source genetics from out-of-state seed banks would lose access to interstate seed shipments after November 12, 2026 — unless the Lawful Hemp Protection Act or another bill passes. This is a distinct compliance risk from the hemp-derived product ban. Oklahoma cultivators should audit their genetics supply chain now: any seed shipments from other states after November 12 could expose the recipient and sender to federal charges. Establishing in-state tissue culture or clone sources before the deadline is the safest path if Congress does not act.

source.name: MJBizDaily — Why the federal hemp ban threatens cannabis seed banks and your favorite genetics (July 2026)

items.headline: Alabama ADPH Hearing on State Rescheduling Concluded July 22 — Decision Expected After August 5 Written Comment Deadline
items.summary: The Alabama Department of Public Health held the scheduled July 22 public hearing on whether to adopt the federal Schedule III rescheduling of marijuana under state law. Testimony was received from members of the public and government agencies. Conservative activists registered objections; only one speaker supported aligning with the federal change. Written public comments are accepted through August 5, 2026, after which ADPH must make its decision. Alabama's state health officer indicated the state 'fully intends' to follow the federal change but needs to work out implementation details.
items.whyItMatters: This confirms that federal Schedule III rescheduling does not automatically update state drug schedules — each state must take its own action. Alabama illustrates a state pushback process that delays automatic adoption. Oklahoma does not have a similar formal objection mechanism; OMMA has been monitoring the federal change and has issued guidance that 280E relief applies to Oklahoma operators regardless of DEA registration status. This Alabama proceeding is national context and does not directly affect Oklahoma OMMA operations.

source.name: Alabama Reflector — ADPH holds hearing on marijuana rescheduling (July 22, 2026)

secondarySource.name: WSFA 12 News — Alabama ADPH holds public hearing on possible marijuana reclassification

items.headline: CONFIRMED: DEA 60-Day Window Extends to Monday June 29 — Saturday Rule; One Final Business Day for Form 225 Filers
items.summary: The 60th calendar day from the April 28 Federal Register publication falls on Saturday June 27. Standard federal rules shift the filing deadline to the next federal business day — Monday June 29, 2026. Manatt, Phelps & Phillips confirmed this: 'filings submitted on the next federal business day will be treated as timely.' This is NOT a formal DEA regulatory extension; it is the routine procedural rule that weekend deadlines roll to Monday. The dispensary-specific portal (mmapplication.diversion.dea.gov) closed June 22 and is unaffected. For cultivators, manufacturers, labs, and distributors: Monday June 29 is the effective last day to file DEA Form 225 (codes 7362/7353/7386) at deadiversion.usdoj.gov. Retain timestamped confirmation. Monday is also the first day of the broader-rescheduling ALJ hearing — file early morning.
items.whyItMatters: Oklahoma cultivators and processors who did not file Form 225 by Friday June 26 have one confirmed final opportunity — Monday June 29 — before the safe-harbor expedited window closes. OBNDD's mandatory registration directive (May 8, 2026) is unchanged; filing by June 29 preserves the right to continue operations during DEA's six-month review. Do not confuse this Saturday-rule extension with the circulating social media claim that 'DEA formally extended the 60-day window' — that rumor (a regulatory extension) remains false. The procedural extension to Monday is real, confirmed by law firms, and is available now.

source.name: Manatt, Phelps & Phillips — DOJ Reclassifies Medical Marijuana (confirms June 29 extension)

secondarySource.name: Caldwell Law — Cannabis Regulatory Update (confirms Saturday-rule shift to June 29)

items.headline: DEA Reschedules State Medical Marijuana & FDA-Approved Products to Schedule III
items.summary: Acting AG Todd Blanche issued a final order moving (1) marijuana in FDA-approved drug products and (2) marijuana under a qualifying state medical license from Schedule I to Schedule III, using treaty-implementation authority (21 U.S.C. 811(d)(1)) without traditional notice-and-comment. Recreational/adult-use, unlicensed marijuana, and synthetically derived THC remain Schedule I. A parallel expedited rulemaking on broader rescheduling was simultaneously initiated.
items.whyItMatters: Most consequential federal cannabis action in decades — creates new DEA registration duties, 280E tax relief, and treaty-compliance obligations for thousands of operators.

source.name: DOJ Press Release

items.headline: DEA Registration Now Required — Expedited Window Closes ~June 26–27, 2026
items.summary: Dispensary portal opened April 29, 2026 at mmapplication.diversion.dea.gov ($794/yr, PayPal only) — OMMA states registration is OPTIONAL for dispensaries; the dedicated dispensary portal CLOSED June 22, 2026 per law firms Fennemore Craig and Foley Hoag LLP, 4 days before the 60-day Federal Register window closes June 26–27. Dedicated DEA application forms for Manufacturer, Bulk Manufacturer (cultivator/grower), Analytical Lab, and Distributor are NOT yet live as of 2026-06-22; a DEA Diversion Control notice (~May 11) said forms are 'coming in the coming weeks,' and the dedicated portal has not launched. The parallel pathway is DEA Form 225 with codes 7362/7353/7386 (fees: $3,699/yr manufacturer; $1,850/yr distributor; $296/yr lab). OBNDD states manufacturers and distributors are REQUIRED to register; enforcement grace period runs to January 1, 2027. The expedited 60-day window closes ~June 26–27 (60 days from April 28 Federal Register publication).
items.whyItMatters: Every cultivator, processor, lab, distributor, and dispensary faces a new federal registration layer. Dispensaries' dedicated portal CLOSED June 22 — dispensaries that missed must contact DEA directly at deadiversion.usdoj.gov before June 26–27. Manufacturers/cultivators must use Form 225 now with 2 days remaining as of June 24 — OBNDD says non-registration risks sanctions. Missing the expedited window means operating without safe-harbor during a six-month DEA review.

source.name: Marijuana Moment

secondarySource.name: OMMA — dispensary instructions

items.headline: OBNDD Mandates DEA Registration for OMMA Manufacturers & Distributors — Deadline ~June 27; Grace Period to Jan 1, 2027
items.summary: OBNDD Director Donnie Anderson directed that every OMMA registrant who is a "distributor or manufacturer" must obtain federal DEA registration. Applying within 60 days of the April 28 publication (~June 27, 2026) lets a business keep operating under state authority during DEA's six-month review. Anderson warned that failure "could result in OBNDD administrative sanctions up to and including the potential revocation" of OBNDD registrations, with an enforcement grace period running until January 1, 2027. The DEA application has seven sections and costs approximately $3,699 for manufacturers and $1,850 for distributors (via Form 225).
items.whyItMatters: OBNDD's requirement reaches ALL licensed manufacturers (growers) and distributors, not just dispensaries; growers who miss it risk losing the OBNDD manufacturer registration that underpins their OMMA grower license.

source.name: Marijuana Moment

items.headline: Oklahoma Moratorium Extended to 2028 — HB 3143 Signed May 7; HB 3144 Cultivation Cap Pending Confirmation
items.summary: Governor Kevin Stitt signed HB 3143 into law on May 7, 2026, extending Oklahoma's new-license moratorium from August 1, 2026 to August 1, 2028; the bill also prohibits transfer of any license currently under investigation. Companion HB 3144 — which would cap total cultivation licenses at 2,500 statewide — passed the House (82–15); its enactment status remains unconfirmed as of June 26, 2026 — HB 3144 does not appear on Governor Stitt's published 2026 veto list (oklahoma.gov/governor/newsroom/2026-veto-page.html), but an explicit signed/enacted confirmation has not been located in primary sources. The Oklahoma legislature adjourned sine die on May 14, 2026, closing the 2026 session. At House passage Oklahoma had 2,164 active grows, 686 processors, 1,421 dispensaries, and 58 transporters. The moratorium applies only to new licenses; existing licensees in good standing may still open new locations or transfer licenses with OMMA approval.
items.whyItMatters: The two-year moratorium extension is now law — no new cultivation or other cannabis licenses will be issued until at least August 2028. Existing grow licenses become scarcer and more valuable as transferable assets. If HB 3144 was enacted before sine die adjournment, the 2,500 cap locks the total count near its current level.

source.name: MJBizDaily — Oklahoma extends moratorium

secondarySource.name: Marijuana Moment

items.headline: Schedule III + Single Convention Activates Five New Compliance Burdens for Cultivators
items.summary: Because the rescheduling order is grounded in treaty-implementation authority, it increases — not reduces — cultivator compliance burdens: (1) SSNs for all personnel with controlled-substance access; (2) disclosure of upstream supplier DEA numbers; (3) DEA on-demand unscheduled inspection authority; (4) a crop purchase-and-resale mechanism where DEA serves as exclusive purchaser at a nominal price before crops can leave the facility; (5) parallel federal recordkeeping including quota reporting and import/export permits. The 2026 cultivator administrative fee is $113/kg on the 6,675 kg aggregate quota.
items.whyItMatters: Operators who assumed "rescheduling = deregulation" face a compliance shock; the DEA purchase-resale mechanism restructures the cultivation supply chain.

source.name: Cannabis Tech

items.headline: D.C. Circuit Cases 26-1106/1130/1136: SAM/NDASA Brief Filed June 4 — Nebraska/Indiana/Idaho Brief FILED June 26 (Marijuana Causes Psychiatric Harm; D'Souza to Testify) — Govt Response July 2; Louisiana Withdrew
items.summary: Three consolidated petition sets challenge the April 28 order: (1) Smart Approaches to Marijuana (SAM) + NDASA, filed May 4 — opening brief filed June 4; (2) AGs of Nebraska, Indiana, and Idaho, filed May 22 — opening brief CONFIRMED FILED June 26 per court deadline — states argue marijuana 'causes our citizens psychiatric harm and is linked to increases in homelessness, traffic accidents, illegal drug trafficking, and other crime'; states designated Dr. Deepak Cyril D'Souza (Yale Center for the Science of Cannabis) as expert witness on abuse liability; government (DOJ) response due July 2 (Louisiana initially co-filed but AG Liz Murrill sought dismissal May 29, leaving only Nebraska and Indiana as state-AG petitioners); (3) a coalition of five 'aggrieved persons' including addiction-treatment organizations (New Directions) and others, filed May 28. Arguments include: exceeds AG authority, violates APA notice-and-comment, implicates the major questions doctrine, and relies on a possibly unconstitutional ALJ structure. All petitioner groups seek both a stay and full vacatur. On June 9, NDASA + MMJ International Holdings + MMJ BioPharma Cultivation + MMJ BioPharma Labs separately filed a formal stay motion in Case 26-1136. No stay has been granted as of June 28, 2026.
items.whyItMatters: A stay could freeze the entire order — reverting state medical marijuana to Schedule I and nullifying registration, 280E relief, and research expansion. The Nebraska/Indiana brief (~June 26) and government response (July 2) are the next immediate court milestones; any subsequent stay ruling is the key risk event for Oklahoma operators.

source.name: NORML

secondarySource.name: Cannabis Business Times — 3 States Challenge

items.headline: NDASA + MMJ BioPharma File D.C. Circuit Stay Motion — First Formal Stay Request in Case 26-1136
items.summary: On June 9, 2026, the National Drug and Alcohol Screening Association (NDASA), MMJ International Holdings, MMJ BioPharma Cultivation, and MMJ BioPharma Labs jointly filed a motion in D.C. Circuit Case No. 26-1136 seeking a stay of Attorney General Order No. 6754-2026 (the April 28 medical-marijuana rescheduling order). The petitioners argue (1) the order bypassed required notice-and-comment rulemaking; (2) the AG lacked authority to act without completing the statutory DEA hearing; and (3) MMJ International/MMJ BioPharma — which invested more than $10 million and nearly a decade developing cannabinoid pharmaceuticals through FDA/DEA channels — suffer commercial harm from state-licensed marijuana operators now competing under the same Schedule III classification without equivalent safety review. Supporting the stay motion, Harvard Medical School neuroscientist Dr. Bertha K. Madras filed an expert declaration (also June 9) warning the D.C. Circuit that rescheduling poses 'substantial' and 'unacceptably high' public health risks — particularly for adolescents and unborn children — and that marijuana does not meet the standards required of a medicine. This is a distinct stay motion from the June 4 SAM/NDASA opening brief. Petitioners may also seek emergency relief before the June 29 broader-rescheduling ALJ hearing. No court ruling has issued as of June 19, 2026.
items.whyItMatters: This is the first formal motion in Case 26-1136 asking the D.C. Circuit to actually halt the order. A granted stay would revert state medical marijuana to Schedule I, nullify all DEA registrations filed under the expedited pathway, and undo 280E relief. Oklahoma operators filing DEA registration before the June 26–27 window preserves the safe-harbor regardless of litigation outcome; the risk of not registering (OBNDD sanctions, license jeopardy) is immediate and certain, while stay risk is real but not yet realized.

source.name: Marijuana Herald — NDASA/MMJ Ask Court to Pause Rescheduling

secondarySource.name: Marijuana Moment — Drug Testing Industry Asks Court to Pause

items.headline: Broader-Rescheduling ALJ Hearing Set for June 29 – July 15, 2026 — Only Rescheduling Opponents Selected as Participants; Constitutional Challenge Active
items.summary: Alongside the partial rescheduling, DEA withdrew its stalled August 2024 proceeding and issued a new Notice of Hearing for an expedited ALJ proceeding on whether ALL marijuana — including adult-use — should move to Schedule III via notice-and-comment rulemaking. The hearing runs June 29 – July 15, 2026 (July 3 recess) at DEA's Arlington, VA facility. PARTICIPANT SELECTION (June 18–19): DEA released its participant list and ALL 7 selected parties oppose rescheduling: National Drug & Alcohol Screening Association (NDASA); Tennessee Bureau of Investigation; Smart Approaches to Marijuana (SAM); the states of Nebraska, Idaho, Indiana, and Louisiana; DUID Victim Voices; Kenneth Finn, MD; and Phillip A. Drum, PharmD. NORML and all cannabis industry, patient, and reform organizations were excluded — zero pro-reform parties selected. A pre-hearing constitutional challenge is also active: the DOJ formally admitted in MMJ BioPharma Cultivation Inc. v. Bondi (Feb. 27, 2025) that DEA ALJ removal protections are unconstitutional; challengers argue that under Axon v. FTC (2022), subjecting parties to proceedings before an unlawful tribunal is itself irreparable injury. MMJ International announced plans to seek emergency injunctive relief against the hearing. If MMJ files an emergency motion against the hearing, the D.C. Circuit could be forced to rule on the constitutional question within days. No injunction has been granted and the hearing remains on schedule as of June 19, 2026.
items.whyItMatters: The hearing's all-opponent participant roster means the administrative record will be built exclusively by rescheduling opponents — no commercial, patient, or consumer perspective included. This structural imbalance may strengthen D.C. Circuit petitioners' procedural arguments and increases the risk that the broader rescheduling recommendation will oppose Schedule III for adult-use marijuana. For Oklahoma operators, neither the hearing's outcome nor its participant composition affects the April 28 state-medical-marijuana Schedule III order already in effect, nor does it change the June 26–27 registration deadline.

source.name: Gibson Dunn

secondarySource.name: Business of Cannabis — Mounting Legal Challenges

items.headline: DEA Names 7 Anti-Rescheduling Parties for June 29 ALJ Hearing — Zero Pro-Reform Parties Selected
items.summary: DEA released its participant list for the June 29 broader-rescheduling ALJ hearing on approximately June 18–19, 2026, and every selected participant opposes marijuana rescheduling. The 7 selected parties are: National Drug & Alcohol Screening Association (NDASA); Tennessee Bureau of Investigation; Smart Approaches to Marijuana (SAM); the states of Nebraska, Idaho, Indiana, and Louisiana; DUID Victim Voices; Kenneth Finn, MD; and Phillip A. Drum, PharmD. NORML, cannabis industry organizations, patient advocates, and all reform groups that filed notices of intent to participate were excluded. The Federal Register notice had stated DEA would notify selected participants by June 22, 2026; notification went out approximately June 18–19. The hearing runs June 29 – July 15, 2026 at DEA's Arlington, VA facility and determines whether all forms of marijuana should move to Schedule III via notice-and-comment rulemaking.
items.whyItMatters: The administrative hearing record will be built exclusively by rescheduling opponents — no cannabis industry, patient, or consumer perspective will be represented. For Oklahoma operators, this has two implications: (1) the hearing is structured to produce an all-opposition record, likely recommending against Schedule III for adult-use marijuana; and (2) the exclusion of all pro-reform parties strengthens procedural arguments available to D.C. Circuit petitioners challenging the April 28 order, potentially increasing the risk of a court stay or vacatur. This hearing development does NOT affect the immediate June 26–27 registration deadline or OBNDD's mandatory requirement for Oklahoma cultivators and manufacturers.

source.name: Cannabis Business Times — DEA Names 7 Anti-Rescheduling Parties, Zero Pro

secondarySource.name: Marijuana Moment — DEA Picks Only Opponents for Hearing

items.headline: Chief ALJ Derek Julius Signs Scheduling Order — June 29 Hearing Explicitly Limited to Adult-Use 'Remainder'; State Medical Marijuana Already Done; Prehearing Deadlines June 24
items.summary: DEA Chief Administrative Law Judge Derek Julius signed a 12-page scheduling order on June 19, 2026, setting the procedural roadmap for the June 29–July 15 broader-rescheduling hearing. Key provisions: (1) government attorneys must file notices of appearance by June 22; (2) designated parties must file prehearing statements (≤25 pages each) by June 24; (3) parties must exchange proposed exhibits by June 24–25; (4) the ALJ will issue a detailed hearing schedule by June 24; (5) the hearing recesses July 3 and reconvenes July 6. CRITICAL SCOPE CLARIFICATION: The order explicitly states 'the scope of this hearing is not to discuss the rescheduling of medical products approved by the Food and Drug Administration that contain marijuana and of medical marijuana products already regulated by the states, which has already occurred' and that 'the narrow issue in this matter is whether the remainder of marijuana, as defined in the CSA, should be transferred from its current place on schedule I of the list of controlled substances to schedule III.' The government carries the burden of proof as proponent of the rule.
items.whyItMatters: The ALJ's explicit scope language provides official confirmation that the June 29 hearing does not threaten the April 28 Schedule III status of Oklahoma state-licensed medical marijuana operators — that rescheduling is already done and is not at issue in the administrative proceeding. The hearing determines only whether adult-use/recreational marijuana also moves to Schedule III. For OMMA-licensed cultivators and dispensaries, this means the DEA registration obligation, 280E exemption, and all compliance burdens from the April 28 order are NOT on the line at this hearing. D.C. Circuit litigation remains a separate and real risk. Oklahoma operators' immediate priority remains filing DEA registration — dispensary portal CLOSED June 22; cultivators/manufacturers must use Form 225 before June 26–27 (2 days remaining as of June 24).

source.name: Marijuana Moment — DEA Judge Issues Order Laying Out Process For Hearing This Month

items.headline: NORML Files Emergency Reconsideration After DEA Excludes All Pro-Reform Groups from June 29 ALJ Hearing
items.summary: Following the DEA's release of the June 29 hearing participant list — which includes only rescheduling opponents — NORML filed an emergency request for reconsideration with the DEA on approximately June 20, 2026, arguing that excluding all pro-reform parties from the broader-rescheduling ALJ hearing violates the public interest. NORML contended that the exclusion 'would cause immediate and irreparable procedural prejudice, impair the completeness and balance of the administrative record, and disserve the public interest in a full and reasoned proceeding concerning the federal legal status of marijuana.' The American Trade Association for Cannabis and Hemp (ATACH) — also excluded — called the selection 'deeply disappointing' and stated it would continue advocacy through the Coalition for Cannabis Scheduling Reform. The DEA had already issued formal participant notifications on June 22; as of June 23, 2026, no public DEA response to NORML's reconsideration has been issued and the June 29 hearing remains on schedule with only the seven anti-rescheduling participants.
items.whyItMatters: NORML's emergency reconsideration confirms that the all-opponent participant roster is being formally challenged through administrative channels, not just commentary. If the DEA denies reconsideration — the likely outcome given notifications already issued — NORML and excluded industry groups may escalate to the D.C. Circuit, adding new procedural claims to the consolidated rescheduling challenges (26-1106/1130/1136). For Oklahoma operators, this does NOT affect the already-effective Schedule III status for state-licensed medical marijuana (Chief ALJ Julius's June 19 order explicitly excludes that from the hearing's scope). The June 29 hearing and its one-sided record remain a risk factor for the future of broader adult-use rescheduling only. Oklahoma operators' immediate priority remains filing DEA registration via Form 225 before June 26–27 — 2 days remain as of June 24.

source.name: Marijuana Moment — NORML Asks DEA To Reconsider Its Exclusion from Hearing

secondarySource.name: NORML — DEA Limits Participants in Hearing to Those Opposed (June 19, 2026)

items.headline: 280E Tax Deduction Bar Lifted for Medical Operators — Binding IRS/Treasury Guidance Still Pending
items.summary: State-licensed medical marijuana is no longer Schedule I/II, so operators are no longer subject to the IRC Section 280E deduction disallowance. Treasury/IRS announced April 23, 2026 that formal guidance is forthcoming and expected to treat rescheduling as applying for the full 2026 tax year. As of June 19, 2026 — over seven weeks after the order took effect — no revenue ruling, IRS notice, proposed regulation, or other binding written guidance has been published. Seven House Democrats formally demanded IRS and Treasury expedite guidance on May 28 (see companion item). No retroactive relief for prior years has been announced; recreational-only operators remain fully subject to 280E.
items.whyItMatters: Potentially tens of millions in aggregate annual tax savings for medical operators; however, operators and CPAs are acting on legal interpretation alone until IRS publishes binding guidance. Allocation methodologies for mixed-use operators and retroactivity scope remain unresolved. A May 2026 DOJ lawsuit demanding return of a cannabis MSO's pre-rescheduling 280E refund signals IRS will aggressively contest any pre-2026 retroactive claims.

source.name: U.S. Treasury

items.headline: IRS/Treasury 280E Guidance Still Not Issued — Seven House Democrats Demand Action
items.summary: Seven House Democrats led by Reps. Steven Horsford and Steve Cohen wrote to IRS Commissioner Danny Bisignano and Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent on May 28, 2026, demanding prompt publication of formal 280E guidance following the April 28 medical-marijuana rescheduling order. As of June 19, 2026 — over seven weeks after rescheduling took effect — neither IRS nor Treasury has published a revenue ruling, IRS notice, proposed regulation, or other binding written guidance on how 280E applies to Schedule III medical-marijuana operators. The April 23 Treasury press release (SB0471) announced intent to issue guidance including a transition rule treating relief as applying for the full 2026 taxable year; it is not itself binding. Operators and CPAs are acting on the legal interpretation that 280E does not apply to state-licensed medical activity, but without IRS confirmation of allocation methodologies or treatment of mixed-use operators.
items.whyItMatters: Oklahoma medical-marijuana operators — cultivators, processors, and dispensaries — cannot finalize 2026 tax strategy with certainty until IRS publishes formal guidance. The seven-week gap between the rescheduling order and any binding IRS action leaves operators exposed if Treasury ultimately limits relief in ways not currently anticipated.

source.name: Cannabis Business Times — 7 House Democrats Demand 280E Guidance

secondarySource.name: The Marijuana Herald — Lawmakers Urge IRS and Treasury

items.headline: DOJ Sues TerrAscend for $8.3M in First Known 280E Refund Clawback — IRS Signals Pre-2026 Claims Will Be Contested
items.summary: The U.S. Department of Justice filed suit May 18, 2026 in U.S. District Court in New Jersey seeking $8.3 million plus interest from cannabis MSO TerrAscend USA — the first known instance of the federal government attempting to recover a 280E tax refund proactively claimed by a cannabis operator. TerrAscend filed an amended 2020 tax return in April 2024 claiming $64.2 million in deductions not permitted under Section 280E, and the IRS sent an $8.3M refund check in June 2024. The DOJ complaint alleges the refund was 'erroneous' because IRS's Joint Committee on Taxation did not review it as required and because TerrAscend was not entitled to the deductions. An IRS bulletin issued the same month TerrAscend received the refund had already warned that amended returns seeking 280E relief were 'not entitled to a refund or payment.' TerrAscend deposited the check the same day it arrived and has 'not voluntarily returned' the funds. Across publicly traded cannabis MSOs, total disputed 280E claims total approximately $1.6 billion.
items.whyItMatters: IRS and DOJ have drawn a clear line: pre-rescheduling 280E refund claims for tax years before 2026 will be aggressively contested, not honored. Oklahoma dispensaries and cultivators considering amended returns for 2024, 2023, or earlier years face the same enforcement risk. The April 28 rescheduling order eliminates 280E going forward (January 1, 2026 and after, per expected Treasury guidance), but IRS has signaled it will fight retroactive relief for prior years without formal written authorization — which has still not been published as of June 19, 2026. Do NOT file pre-2026 amended returns without specialized tax counsel.

source.name: Business of Cannabis — DOJ Sues TerrAscend for $8.3M in First Known 280E Refund Clawback

secondarySource.name: MJBizDaily — IRS: Cannabis MSO must return 'erroneous' 280E refund

items.headline: Congress Closes the Hemp Loophole — Delta-8 / THCA / Synthetics Banned Effective Nov 12, 2026
items.summary: The Continuing Appropriations and Extensions Act, 2026 (H.R. 5371 §781), enacted November 12, 2025, rewrote the federal hemp definition: the 0.3% THC threshold now counts total THC including THCA; final consumable products are capped at 0.4 mg total THC per container; intermediates are capped at 0.3% total THC dry-weight post-decarboxylation; cannabinoids not naturally produced by the plant or synthesized outside it are excluded and treated as Schedule I. Products exceeding thresholds after November 12, 2026 revert to Schedule I.
items.whyItMatters: Functionally eliminates the current Delta-8/Delta-10/THCA-flower intoxicating-hemp market unless reformulated or Congress acts before the effective date.

source.name: Arnold & Porter Advisory

items.headline: OMMA Monitors Rescheduling & Confirms 280E Relief Applies Regardless of DEA Registration Status
items.summary: OMMA's official notice confirms it is monitoring DEA rescheduling, that approximately 4,000 OMMA licensees are affected, and that 280E removal applies to Oklahoma medical operators regardless of whether a licensee pursues DEA registration. OMMA cautioned that all information "remains subject to change pending petition outcomes" — a reference to the consolidated D.C. Circuit litigation.
items.whyItMatters: Oklahoma operators get federal tax relief now, but the pending lawsuits are the key wildcard that could reverse it.

source.name: OMMA Official Notice

items.headline: Oklahoma Grower License: Current Compliance Stack — Metrc, OBNDD Registration, 75% Residency, $50K Bond
items.summary: Commercial growers must comply with OMMA rules and hold an OBNDD "Manufacturer" registration. Key requirements: ≥75% Oklahoma-resident ownership; owners ≥25 years old; background checks (nonviolent felony within 2 yrs / any felony within 5 yrs disqualifying); $50,000 surety bond or 5+ years land ownership; not within 1,000 ft of a school; outdoor growers register with the Oklahoma Department of Agriculture; active Metrc seed-to-sale tracking (tags at vegetative stage, movements reported within 24 hrs, sales pushed to Metrc same day). Tier-1 fees range $2,558.30 to $50,000+ by canopy size.
items.whyItMatters: The 75% residency rule is the main anti-foreign-ownership mechanism; post-moratorium applicants will face all of this plus the new DEA registration layer.

source.name: OMMA Grower License

items.headline: Illegal-Grow Crackdown: 51-Defendant Federal Indictment; 1.7M+ Plants Seized Since 2021
items.summary: A multi-agency task force (Oklahoma AG Organized Crime Task Force, OBN/OBNDD, OMMA, ICE/HSI, Cherokee Nation Marshals, National Guard) targets illegal grows, many linked to Chinese nationals. A June 24, 2025 Craig/Mayes County operation seized 40,723 plants and 1,000+ lbs of processed marijuana. In April 2026 a DEA-led Homeland Security Task Force announced a 51-defendant federal indictment over a nationwide black-market conspiracy originating in Oklahoma. OBNDD Director Anderson testified that product from ~85 million plants is unaccounted for. Since 2021: 1.7 million illegal plants seized, 181,000 lbs confiscated, 302 arrests.
items.whyItMatters: Licensed growers compete against massive illegal supply, and OMMA agents are embedded in the criminal task force — administrative investigative powers are being used in prosecutions.

source.name: DEA Press Release

items.headline: OMMA New Testing Laboratory Rules Take Effect June 1, 2026
items.summary: OMMA's updated permanent testing laboratory rules took effect June 1, 2026, the product of a multi-year process directed by HB 4056 (2022) in which seven licensed lab directors met across 15 sessions (90+ hours) and submitted 120 pages of recommendations. The rules amend OAC 442:10-8-1 and OAC 442:10-8-3 and address manufacturer's instructions for use, enrichment steps and quality control sample requirements, inventory tag requirements, and clarifications on yeast/mold and potency-confirmation testing for distillate. A prior set of emergency rule updates for licensed laboratories was approved separately. Stakeholders at the public comment hearing had pressed OMMA for clearer standards and broader lab access to Metrc data.
items.whyItMatters: Cultivators and processors submitting products for third-party testing must now meet the updated standards. Products that fail under new methodology cannot legally enter the distribution chain. Labs out of compliance with the new equipment or procedural rules face potential OMMA enforcement, which could create bottlenecks affecting product release timelines for growers and manufacturers.

source.name: OMMA — New Testing Lab Rules Effective June 1

secondarySource.name: OMMA Laboratory Standardization

items.headline: Oklahoma Cannabis Market in Freefall — Wholesale Prices Down 63%, 6,000+ Grows Shuttered
items.summary: Oklahoma Watch and KGOU (NPR Oklahoma) reported June 2, 2026 that the state's medical cannabis market is in severe contraction. Wholesale prices have fallen from approximately $3,000/lb at peak to $1,100/lb (≈63% decline), making Oklahoma the national price floor at $2.11/gram. A 2023 study found licensed supply exceeded patient demand by roughly 64 to 1. At peak, Oklahoma had nearly 14,000 licensed businesses including ~9,400 growers; more than 6,000 grow locations have since shut down or been abandoned. The industry generated $670M+ in retail sales and $1.1B+ in wholesale transactions in 2025, contributing ~$47M in excise taxes and $60M+ in state and local sales taxes — but revenue and margins are collapsing. Governor Stitt is calling for voters to shut the program down entirely; legislative leadership has resisted abolition.
items.whyItMatters: Oklahoma cultivators and processors face existential margin pressure from oversupply and falling wholesale prices at the same moment new DEA registration costs ($3,699/yr for manufacturers) are being imposed. Dispensary customers face the same squeeze from the retail side. The license moratorium extension (HB 3143, signed May 2026) prevents new entrants but does nothing to reduce the existing licensed supply overhang. CAKE and its wholesale dispensary customers operate in this environment — the cost and compliance burden of DEA registration compounds an already-stressed market.

source.name: Oklahoma Watch — Growers Say Cannabis Bonanza Is Going to Pot

secondarySource.name: KGOU NPR Oklahoma — Same Report

items.headline: OMMA Mandatory Recall — All Products Tested by Greenleaf Labs LLC Between April 2023 and July 2025 (~19,000 Samples)
items.summary: OMMA issued a mandatory recall of all medical marijuana products tested by Greenleaf Labs LLC (license LAAA-MP4O-T1EE) during April 2023 through July 2025, after finding the laboratory inaccurately calculated yeast and mold content throughout that period. Approximately 19,000 samples across multiple Oklahoma licensees are affected — among the largest lab-testing recalls by sample count in Oklahoma OMMA history. Operators whose products were tested by Greenleaf Labs during the affected window must identify, quarantine, and address affected inventory per OMMA recall procedures. Item date is approximate; confirm the exact recall issue date and current scope at the OMMA source URL.
items.whyItMatters: Oklahoma cultivators and processors who used Greenleaf Labs for testing between April 2023 and July 2025 face mandatory recall obligations covering years of historical product. CAKE should immediately audit its testing records against Greenleaf Labs' license number (LAAA-MP4O-T1EE) — any product in that scope cannot legally be sold and must be quarantined. The recall follows the June 1, 2026 effective date of OMMA's new lab testing rules and reflects an elevated OMMA enforcement posture on testing integrity.

source.name: OMMA — Mandatory Recall: Greenleaf Labs LLC

items.headline: Oklahoma SQ 837 (Adult-Use Legalization Initiative) Will Not Appear on November 2026 Ballot — Petitioners Failed to Gather Signatures
items.summary: Proponents of Oklahoma State Question 837 — a citizen initiative that would have legalized adult-use recreational marijuana in Oklahoma — failed to gather the required petition signatures by the filing deadline. SQ 837 will not appear on the November 2026 ballot. Oklahoma voters previously rejected SQ 820 (adult-use legalization) in March 2023. With SQ 837 confirmed as non-qualifying, Oklahoma's cannabis market will remain medical-only through at least the 2028 election cycle barring a legislative referral.
items.whyItMatters: For CAKE and its wholesale dispensary customers, the failure of SQ 837 removes the only near-term market-expansion catalyst — adult-use legalization would have substantially broadened the consumer base and potentially relieved oversupply pressure. Oklahoma will remain a medical-only state for the foreseeable future. Business plans premised on near-term adult-use expansion in Oklahoma should not be advanced based on a 2026 ballot measure; the next realistic opportunity would be a 2028 initiative campaign.

source.name: Ballotpedia — Oklahoma State Question 837

items.headline: DEA Begins On-Site Inspections at Cannabis Businesses That Registered Under Schedule III
items.summary: The Drug Enforcement Administration began conducting on-site inspections at state-licensed cannabis businesses that applied for federal DEA registration following the April 28 rescheduling order. The Mississippi Medical Marijuana Association reported that two of its dispensary members were among the first in the country visited after submitting applications through the DEA Medical Marijuana Dispensary Registration Portal. High Street Dispensary in Jackson, MS (owner David Fowler) hosted 5–6 DEA representatives for approximately six hours; Fowler described the agents as 'very cordial' and 'did not come in overbearing,' noting the agents said they were 'new to this whole process too' and approached the visit as collaborative and inquisitive. Both visited dispensaries were told they were among the first in the country to receive inspections. DEA inspections are a standard step in processing new Schedule III registration applications — agents verify facility compliance, security, records, and personnel information provided in the application.
items.whyItMatters: This is the first confirmed ground-level evidence that DEA's Schedule III oversight is now operational — registration is not a paper exercise but triggers physical federal presence at the facility. Oklahoma dispensaries and cultivators/manufacturers who file registration applications (dispensary portal by June 22; Form 225 by June 26–27) should expect an in-person DEA visit as part of the review process. Before filing, operators must ensure their facilities, employee documentation (SSNs for all personnel with controlled-substance access), security systems, record-keeping, and chain-of-custody logs are in order. The collaborative tone at these first visits is consistent with DEA's standard registration process — Schedule III inspections under 21 U.S.C. 880 include authority to enter at any time without advance notice. The inspection news does not change OBNDD's mandatory registration directive for manufacturers and distributors; it does mean non-registration avoidance strategies based on 'avoiding DEA visibility' are now tested against reality.

source.name: Marijuana Moment — DEA Begins On-Site Inspections at Marijuana Businesses

items.headline: Oklahoma HB 4454 Signed — Edibles Restricted to 10mg/Serving, 100mg/Package; Cartoon Shapes and Minor-Targeted Marketing Banned; Effective November 1, 2026
items.summary: Governor Kevin Stitt signed HB 4454 into law on May 7, 2026, effective November 1, 2026. The law limits THC in medical marijuana edibles to 10mg per serving and 100mg per package (20mg per drink container); bans packaging in shapes resembling humans, animals, cartoons, or commercially available candy; and prohibits marketing that appeals to minors. The bill was signed the same day as HB 3143 (license moratorium extension to August 2028).
items.whyItMatters: Oklahoma dispensaries must audit their entire edibles inventory for compliance before November 1, 2026 — approximately 4.5 months away. Processors and manufacturers producing edibles for the Oklahoma market must reformulate products exceeding 10mg/serving and redesign any non-compliant packaging before that date. Products in licensed inventory after November 1 that exceed the per-serving or per-package THC caps, or feature banned shapes, are out of compliance. Given OMMA's active enforcement posture (6,000+ inspections, 704 products recalled), this is an enforcement-ready standard. Dispensary buyers should flag non-compliant edible SKUs for reformulation or discontinuation now.

source.name: BillTrack50 — HB 4454 Oklahoma 2026

secondarySource.name: Legilist — HB 4454 Oklahoma 2026 Regular Session

items.headline: Rohrabacher-Blumenauer Rider Expired July 2025 — RESTORED by P.L. 119-74 (Signed January 23, 2026); Anti-Rescheduling Block Excluded from Final Appropriations
items.summary: The Rohrabacher-Blumenauer rider (also known as the Joyce-Leahy amendment) — which since FY2015 had prohibited the DOJ from spending appropriated funds to interfere with individuals acting in compliance with state medical marijuana laws — was excluded from the 'One Big Beautiful Bill Act' signed July 4, 2025. Since that date, DOJ has no appropriations-based prohibition on prosecuting state-legal medical cannabis operators under the CSA. This removes the practical backstop that had protected OMMA-compliant operators from federal prosecution even without a federal registration. UPDATE (P.L. 119-74, SIGNED JANUARY 23, 2026): The FY2026 Commerce, Justice, Science and Related Agencies Appropriations Act (P.L. 119-74) restored the medical marijuana state protections rider via Section 531, ending the ~6.5-month gap (July 4, 2025 – January 23, 2026). Oklahoma is covered; Idaho, Kansas, Nebraska, and American Samoa are explicitly excluded. The House Appropriations version of Section 607 that would have blocked DEA from rescheduling cannabis was removed from the final bicameral agreement.
items.whyItMatters: UPDATED — RIDER RESTORED: The rider was absent July 4, 2025 – January 23, 2026 (~6.5 months). Since P.L. 119-74 was signed January 23, 2026, Oklahoma OMMA-licensed operators again have Congressional appropriations-based protection: DOJ cannot use appropriated funds to target OMMA-compliant operators for activities lawful under Oklahoma medical marijuana law. However, this does not replace DEA registration: (1) the rider bars DOJ enforcement spending but does not grant positive federal compliance authority or registration status; (2) the OBNDD mandatory DEA registration obligation (enforcement from January 1, 2027) is independently imposed by Oklahoma state law; (3) the rider provides no 280E tax relief; (4) if the April 28 rescheduling order is stayed or vacated by the D.C. Circuit, the rider's medical marijuana state protections would still apply independently as a separate protection layer. The exclusion of Section 607 (rescheduling block) from P.L. 119-74 means Congress has affirmatively declined to block the April 28 rescheduling order through the appropriations process.

source.name: Business of Cannabis — US State Medical Cannabis Markets in 'Dire Jeopardy'

secondarySource.name: MG Magazine — June 26 DEA Registration Deadline Looms

items.headline: ALJ Issues Detailed Testimony Schedule for June 29–July 15 Hearing — All 7 Anti-Rescheduling Parties Scheduled; States Testify July 14
items.summary: Chief Administrative Law Judge Derek Julius issued a detailed testimony schedule on June 24, 2026, confirming the broader-rescheduling ALJ hearing proceeds as planned. The full schedule: Government (DOJ) testifies June 29; NDASA July 2; Smart Approaches to Marijuana (SAM) July 6; DUID Victim Voices July 7; Kenneth Finn MD July 8; Tennessee Bureau of Investigation July 10; Phillip A. Drum PharmD July 13; States of Nebraska, Idaho, Indiana, and Louisiana July 14. The hearing runs June 29 through July 15 with a July 3 recess reconvening July 6, at DEA's facility at 700 Army Navy Drive, Arlington, VA. Marijuana Moment sent a letter to Chief ALJ Julius requesting public livestream access; the ALJ has not responded as of June 25, 2026. No emergency injunction or stay motion has been granted and the hearing remains on schedule.
items.whyItMatters: The detailed testimony schedule confirms the June 29 hearing is proceeding — dispelling social media claims that the hearing was cancelled or that MMJ International obtained an injunction to stop it. For Oklahoma operators, the hearing covers ONLY adult-use/recreational marijuana; Chief ALJ Julius explicitly confirmed in his June 19 scheduling order that state-licensed medical marijuana rescheduling 'has already occurred' and is NOT at issue. OMMA-licensed cultivators' and dispensaries' Schedule III status, 280E relief, and registration obligation are unaffected by the hearing's outcome. The testimony schedule also shows the anti-rescheduling state coalition (Nebraska, Idaho, Indiana, Louisiana) presents July 14 — the same briefs are also pending in the D.C. Circuit, where the court challenge remains the more consequential threat to the April 28 order.

source.name: Marijuana Moment — DEA Judge Sets Testimony Schedule for Marijuana Rescheduling Hearing

secondarySource.name: Marijuana Herald — DEA Judge Sets Schedule for Hearing Beginning June 29

items.headline: ALJ Bars Livestream from June 29 Hearing; DEA Resists Testimony from Its Own Official on Marijuana Harms
items.summary: Chief Administrative Law Judge Derek Julius's June 18, 2026 Preliminary Order bars all recording devices from the hearing room and lobby — the June 29 broader-rescheduling ALJ hearing will not be livestreamed. In-person attendance at DEA's 700 Army Navy Drive, Arlington, VA facility is the only way to observe the proceedings. Marijuana Moment counsel formally requested reconsideration on June 23, arguing the DEA had allowed livestreaming of the prior (Biden-era) cancelled hearing; Americans for Safe Access and partner advocates sent a separate letter; as of June 25-26 no reversal had been issued by Chief ALJ Julius. In a separate procedural development reported by Marijuana Moment on June 25, DEA resisted testimony from a DEA agency official linked to a report on marijuana's harms — an unusual stance given the agency presents as the first witness on June 29 as proponent of the rescheduling rule.
items.whyItMatters: Oklahoma cultivators and dispensaries monitoring the June 29–July 15 hearing — which addresses only adult-use rescheduling and does not affect the already-effective Schedule III status of state-licensed medical marijuana — cannot follow proceedings remotely. The DEA's resistance to its own official's testimony on marijuana's harms is a notable procedural signal: the agency is managing what enters the administrative record even on the government's own side. Chief ALJ Julius's scope language (June 19) already confirms the hearing does not touch OMMA operators' current Schedule III status, 280E relief, or registration obligation; these procedural developments reinforce that the hearing is likely to produce a selective administrative record, which will matter more for D.C. Circuit litigation risk than for Oklahoma operators' immediate compliance choices.

source.name: Marijuana Moment — DEA Resists Testimony From Agency Official on Marijuana Harms

secondarySource.name: Marijuana Moment — DEA Judge Pressed to Allow Livestream Access

items.headline: DEA's Own Witnesses Will Testify About Marijuana's Medical Benefits at June 29 Hearing
items.summary: New pre-hearing filings reveal the DEA's own government witnesses at the June 29 broader-rescheduling hearing will highlight marijuana's medical benefits. Dr. Corey Burchman, a New Hampshire pain management physician with 30+ years of experience, will testify that 'medical marijuana provides a medical benefit to pain patients.' Dominic Chiapperino, director of the controlled substance staff at FDA's Center for Drug Evaluation and Research, will testify about the eight-factor analysis used to assess marijuana's currently accepted medical use — the pharmacological framework underlying the rescheduling determination. The government testifies first on June 29 as proponent of the rescheduling rule.
items.whyItMatters: The DEA's own witness list shows the government will present a pro-medical-benefits case at the June 29 hearing even though all 7 designated participants oppose rescheduling. This suggests DEA is building a formal medical-record rebuttal for the administrative record. For Oklahoma operators, Chief ALJ Julius's June 19 order confirmed the hearing addresses only adult-use marijuana — Oklahoma state-licensed medical marijuana's Schedule III status is not at issue at the hearing.

source.name: Marijuana Moment — DEA Will Highlight Testimony On Marijuana's Medical Benefits In Rescheduling Hearing

items.headline: SAFER Banking Act Reintroduced with Bipartisan Support in Senate and House — June 25, 2026
items.summary: Bipartisan lawmakers reintroduced the SAFER Banking Act in both chambers on June 25, 2026, the eve of the DEA registration deadline. Senate sponsors: Sens. Jeff Merkley (D-OR), Lisa Murkowski (R-AK), Elizabeth Warren (D-MA), and Steve Daines (R-MT). House sponsors: Reps. Jim Himes (D-CT), Warren Davidson (R-OH), Nydia Velázquez (D-NY), Brian Mast (R-FL), Lou Correa (D-CA), Guy Reschenthaler (R-PA), and Dina Titus (D-NV). The 2026 version would bar federal regulators from penalizing financial institutions for banking state-legal cannabis businesses, and explicitly extends safe harbor to ancillary businesses including landlords, technology vendors, accounting firms, and security providers.
items.whyItMatters: Oklahoma dispensaries and cultivators continue to face banking access challenges — accounts closed without warning, cash-only operations, and limited payment processing. The SAFER Banking Act has passed the House multiple times but has stalled in the Senate. Reintroduction is not enactment; operators should not change banking arrangements based on a bill filing alone. However, the bipartisan co-sponsors and timing alongside Schedule III rescheduling may give the bill better prospects than prior sessions.

source.name: MJBizDaily — Marijuana banking protections reintroduced in Congress

secondarySource.name: Cannabis Business Times — 8 Bipartisan House Lawmakers Reintroduce SAFE Banking Act

items.headline: Chief ALJ Refuses Outside Livestream Requests; Marijuana Moment Escalates to DEA Administrator Cole; Rep. Cohen Joins Push — No Reversal Before Monday Opening
items.summary: Chief Administrative Law Judge Derek Julius stated he would not consider outside parties' requests to livestream the June 29 hearing. Marijuana Moment then took the request directly to DEA Administrator Terrance Cole. On June 27, Rep. Steve Cohen (D-TN) separately sent a letter to DEA Administrator Cole calling the proceedings 'historic' and requesting that the public be able to follow them in real time. DEA announced it will make a transcript available after the hearing concludes, but Marijuana Moment's counsel argued a post-hearing transcript does not serve the public interest in daily real-time access. As of June 27, no reversal has been issued; the June 29 hearing will proceed with no public livestream.
items.whyItMatters: The June 29 hearing — covering only adult-use marijuana rescheduling, not Oklahoma state-licensed medical marijuana's existing Schedule III status — will be entirely closed to remote observation. In-person attendance at 700 Army Navy Drive, Arlington, VA is the only option. Chief ALJ Julius's refusal to accept outside-party requests confirms the hearing is a closed administrative proceeding, not a public forum. The escalation to the DEA Administrator is notable; if Cole overrules Julius before Monday morning, that would be an unusual administrative development worth monitoring.

source.name: Marijuana Moment — Congressman And Other Media Outlets Join Push For DEA To Livestream Rescheduling Hearing

secondarySource.name: Marijuana Moment — Takes Livestreaming Ask Directly To DEA Head After Judge Says He Won't Consider Request

items.headline: Rescheduling Opponents File Pre-Hearing Briefs June 26 — Nebraska/Idaho/Indiana: Marijuana Causes Psychiatric Harm; Dr. D'Souza to Testify; Louisiana Dropped from D.C. Circuit
items.summary: On June 26, parties opposing marijuana rescheduling filed prehearing statements with the DEA ALJ ahead of the June 29 broader-rescheduling hearing. The states of Nebraska, Idaho, and Indiana filed a joint statement arguing marijuana 'causes our citizens psychiatric harm and is linked to increases in homelessness, traffic accidents, illegal drug trafficking, and other crime.' The states designated Dr. Deepak Cyril D'Souza — inaugural director of the Yale Center for the Science of Cannabis and Cannabinoids — as their expert witness on abuse liability. Separately, the June 26 D.C. Circuit briefing deadline was met: the states filed their opening brief on that schedule. Louisiana AG Liz Murrill had previously sought dismissal from the D.C. Circuit case (May 29), leaving only Nebraska and Indiana as state-AG petitioners. The government (DOJ/DEA) response brief is due July 2, 2026.
items.whyItMatters: The prehearing arguments confirm the administrative record at the June 29–July 15 hearing will be built entirely around psychiatric harm, criminal justice, and traffic safety data — no commercial, patient, or agricultural perspective will be in the record. For Oklahoma OMMA operators, this does not affect the already-effective Schedule III status for state-licensed medical marijuana (Chief ALJ Julius's June 19 order explicitly excluded this from the hearing scope). However, the D.C. Circuit briefing is now complete on the petitioner side; the government's July 2 response and any subsequent stay ruling represent the continued risk that the April 28 order could be halted. Harris Sliwoski's Canna Law Blog noted a one-sided administrative record may weaken the final rule's durability under judicial review.

source.name: Marijuana Moment — Marijuana Opponents Preview Arguments For Next Week's Rescheduling Hearing In New Filings

secondarySource.name: MJBizDaily — Louisiana drops out of federal marijuana rescheduling challenge

items.headline: DEA 60-Day Expedited Registration Window Officially Closes June 29; Broader-Rescheduling ALJ Hearing Opens as Scheduled — No Stay Granted
items.summary: The DEA's 60-day expedited registration window for Schedule III medical marijuana operators officially closes June 29, 2026 — the effective final day under the Saturday-rule shift (60th calendar day was June 27; confirmed by Manatt, Phelps & Phillips). Operators who filed Form 225 with drug codes 7362/7353/7386 by today hold safe-harbor operating authority during DEA's six-month review. The dedicated dispensary portal had closed June 22; no dedicated manufacturer/cultivator/lab/distributor portal launched before the window closed — Form 225 was the only available pathway. Simultaneously, the broader-rescheduling ALJ hearing opened as scheduled at 9:00 a.m. ET at DEA's 700 Army Navy Drive Arlington VA facility; no emergency injunction or stay was granted by any federal court as of June 29. The government (DOJ/DEA) is testifying first on Day 1 per Chief ALJ Julius's June 24 testimony schedule. Operators who applied may still expect an on-site DEA inspection as part of the review process — consistent with the June 19 Mississippi dispensary visits.

OBNDD's January 1, 2027 enforcement grace period is not a substitute — it only delays when OBNDD begins active enforcement, not whether DEA's safe-harbor applies during review. Operators can still apply outside the expedited window at deadiversion.usdoj.gov — late filing is better than not filing, but carries no safe-harbor guarantee. For Oklahoma dispensaries, OMMA registration remains optional (no OBNDD mandate applies to dispensaries). The ALJ hearing is confirmed underway; its scope is ONLY adult-use/recreational marijuana — the April 28 Schedule III order for state-licensed medical marijuana is not at issue per Chief ALJ Julius's June 19 scope order.

items.headline: Trulieve Becomes First U.S. Cannabis Company to List on NYSE — Schedule III Rescheduling Removes Major Exchange Barrier
items.summary: Trulieve Cannabis Corp. announced June 5, 2026 that its shares were approved for listing on the New York Stock Exchange; the stock began trading under ticker TRLV on June 10, 2026, making Trulieve the first U.S. plant-touching cannabis company to list on a major U.S. exchange. The listing followed the April 28 rescheduling of state-licensed medical marijuana to Schedule III, which removed the prior regulatory barrier that major U.S. exchanges had cited in refusing to list cannabis operators. Trulieve also executed a corporate restructuring separating its medical operations from mixed-use activities. Curaleaf Holdings and Verano Holdings separately executed reverse stock splits (1-for-3 and 1-for-5 respectively) as steps toward potential uplistings on major exchanges.
items.whyItMatters: Trulieve's NYSE listing is the first direct capital-markets consequence of Schedule III rescheduling — a major exchange has made an affirmative determination that Schedule III status is sufficient to permit listing U.S. plant-touching operators. For CAKE and Oklahoma wholesale dispensary customers, NYSE access matters because it lowers the cost of capital for large multistate operators, which may accelerate national consolidation and put further wholesale pricing pressure on smaller-state operators. Oklahoma operators remain state-licensed only and are not directly NYSE-eligible under current rules, but the shift signals that the equity capital environment for cannabis operators has changed materially since April 28. The listing also serves as a market-level signal that the Schedule III order has sufficient durability that a major exchange — with its own regulatory and reputational exposure — was willing to accept the listing.

items.headline: ALJ Hearing Day 1 (June 29): Government's FDA Witness Testifies Marijuana Safer Than Alcohol and Opioids — SAM Calls Rescheduling 'Solely Political'
items.summary: The broader-rescheduling ALJ hearing opened June 29 at 9:00 a.m. ET at DEA headquarters in Arlington, VA, with the government (DOJ/DEA) presenting as the proponent of the rule. FDA's Dominic Chiapperino — Director of the Controlled Substance Staff at the Center for Drug Evaluation and Research — testified to the eight-factor scheduling analysis, finding marijuana's abuse potential 'less than Schedule I/II substances.' He compared cannabis withdrawal (irritability-level) favorably to alcohol (which carries seizure risk and death-level withdrawal syndrome) and opioids — the government's own affirmative scientific case for Schedule III classification. Dr. Corey Burchman (board-certified pain management physician, NH) began his testimony on Day 2 (June 30), describing how he has transitioned patients from opioids to marijuana. SAM CEO Kevin Sabet (a designated participant scheduled to testify July 6) issued a press statement at an event outside the hearing calling rescheduling 'solely political,' citing cannabis industry campaign donations to Trump. Pro-reform advocates denied participation held a press conference outside the facility. No emergency stay or injunction was granted. The hearing has no livestream; DEA.gov transcript publishes only after proceedings conclude post-July 15.
items.whyItMatters: The government's own FDA scientific witness presented affirmative evidence for Schedule III classification — this is the medical case the government will defend in the D.C. Circuit. Sabet's 'solely political' framing will circulate heavily through July 15 as designated anti-rescheduling participants testify one by one. The hearing information blackout (no livestream, no daily transcript) means any social media or newsletter claims about 'what was revealed today' between July 1 and July 15 are unverifiable until DEA releases the official transcript. Oklahoma operators should not adjust compliance posture based on unverified hearing-day claims. Chief ALJ Julius's June 19 scope order confirmed the hearing addresses only adult-use marijuana — it does not affect Oklahoma state-licensed medical marijuana's Schedule III status or OBNDD registration obligation.
