items.headline: ALJ Hearing Day 2 (June 30): Opponents Attack Government's CAMU Methodology — Five-to-Two-Part Test Switch Introduced 2 Months Before FDA Finished Analysis; Burchman Testifies Marijuana Replaced Opioids for Pain Patients
items.summary: On the second day of the broader-rescheduling ALJ hearing (June 30, 2026), two developments shaped the administrative record. First, Dr. Corey Burchman — a New Hampshire board-certified anesthesiologist and pain management physician who practiced until 2019 — testified that once medical cannabis became available at Dartmouth-Hitchcock Medical Center, he and colleagues would 'avidly use that ability to limit opioids,' with some patients fully transitioning off prescription painkillers. He called cannabis 'extremely helpful in chronic pain patients as a means of analgesia.' Second, and more legally consequential: opponents cross-examined FDA's Dominic Chiapperino about the government's switch from a prior five-part test to a new two-part test for determining 'currently accepted medical use' (CAMU) — the CAMU determination is the legal keystone that classifies marijuana as Schedule III rather than Schedule I. An attorney for the states of Idaho, Indiana, and Nebraska pressed Chiapperino about a 2015 FDA analysis that concluded marijuana did not have CAMU, noting the new two-part test did not exist when that analysis began. Chiapperino acknowledged the new two-part test was introduced in July 2023 — just two months before FDA analysts finished the current analysis. The DOJ's OLC determined in 2024 that the prior five-part test was 'impermissibly narrow,' but opponents argued the midstream methodology change was improper. The government's ALJ attorney said the hearing was not the forum to litigate that dispute. The seven anti-rescheduling hearing participants all had the right to cross-examine the government's witnesses.
items.whyItMatters: The two-part CAMU test controversy is the opponents' sharpest line of attack — it challenges not the policy outcome but the legal methodology used to reach it, which is the kind of procedural argument federal courts evaluate under APA 'arbitrary and capricious' review. If the D.C. Circuit concludes the government improperly changed its CAMU methodology mid-analysis, it could vacate or stay the April 28 Schedule III order. Oklahoma OMMA operators' immediate compliance posture is unchanged: window closed June 29; operators who filed hold safe-harbor; those who did not should apply now via Form 225 at deadiversion.usdoj.gov; OBNDD enforcement begins January 1, 2027. However, compliance directors should monitor the DOJ's July 2 D.C. Circuit response brief for how the government defends the two-part CAMU test switch — this will signal the litigation strength of the April 28 order going forward.

source.name: Marijuana Moment — Opponents Attack Government Analysis Behind Rescheduling Recommendation on Second Day of DEA Hearing

items.headline: DOJ Files D.C. Circuit Response Brief July 2 — Challengers 'Come Nowhere Near' Stay Standard; 'Pocketbook Interests' Attack on NDASA/MMJ Standing
items.summary: The U.S. Department of Justice filed its response brief in consolidated D.C. Circuit rescheduling challenges (Cases 26-1106/1130/1136) on July 2, 2026, opposing petitioners' stay and vacatur motions. Three key legal arguments: (1) STAY STANDARD — Challengers 'come nowhere near' meeting the four-factor standard required for a stay. (2) STANDING — DOJ argued NDASA failed to establish Article III standing, offering only 'generalized speculation about how the rescheduling order might affect the drug-testing industry rather than particularized allegations about how the order has affected specific members of the association.' (3) ZONE OF INTERESTS — DOJ argued petitioners' alleged injuries fall outside the CSA's purpose: Congress enacted the law to protect public health and ensure proper regulation for medical/scientific purposes, 'not to guarantee revenue for drug screeners or protect future market opportunities for cannabinoid-based pharmaceuticals.' DOJ characterized petitioners as invoking 'pocketbook interests served by keeping all marijuana in schedule I.' The D.C. Circuit can now rule on the stay motion at any time following this brief.
items.whyItMatters: The DOJ brief is the government's substantive legal defense of the April 28 Schedule III order in the D.C. Circuit — the forum where the most consequential risk to Oklahoma operators lies. The strong stay-standard framing ('nowhere near'), standing challenge, and 'pocketbook interests' characterization collectively represent a comprehensive government defense of the order. A denied stay means the April 28 order remains in effect with Oklahoma operators' Schedule III status, 280E relief, and DEA registration intact. A granted stay would revert to Schedule I — but DOJ's brief signals strong government confidence in the order's durability. The D.C. Circuit's ruling on the stay motion, which could come at any time, is now the single most consequential pending legal event for Oklahoma operators.

source.name: Marijuana Moment — DOJ Marijuana Lawsuit Filing Cites Drug Testing Industry And Pharma Company 'Pocketbook Interests' In Opposing Rescheduling

secondarySource.name: The Marijuana Herald — DOJ Urges Appeals Court to Reject Attempt to Block Marijuana Rescheduling Order

items.headline: OMMA Launches Quality Assurance Lab Webpage — In-House State Testing Capacity Operational; Authorized by SB 813 (2023)
items.summary: The Oklahoma Medical Marijuana Authority launched a dedicated Quality Assurance Laboratory (QA Lab) webpage on June 29, 2026, signaling that OMMA's in-house QA Lab — authorized by Senate Bill 813 (2023) — is operational. The lab integrates advanced testing capabilities directly into Oklahoma's regulatory framework, reducing reliance on third-party testing data and increasing OMMA's capacity to detect improper testing practices, reporting errors, and product safety risks. OMMA recently hosted the Cannabis Regulators Association (CANNRA) for a lab tour. OMMA will update the webpage as pending accreditations are finalized.
items.whyItMatters: OMMA's in-house QA Lab changes the enforcement calculus for Oklahoma operators: OMMA can now independently re-test products and cross-check third-party lab results rather than relying solely on licensed labs' reported data. This directly addresses the testing integrity failures that drove the Greenleaf Labs recall (approximately 19,000 samples, April 2023–July 2025) and signals elevated OMMA enforcement capability on testing fraud. Cultivators and processors should expect OMMA to use QA Lab results to identify discrepancies and initiate enforcement actions against operators whose products carry inaccurate certifications. The lab's June 29 launch was overshadowed by the simultaneous opening of the ALJ broader-rescheduling hearing.

items.headline: ALJ Hearing Day 4 (July 2): NDASA Testifies Rescheduling Eliminates Federal DOT Drug-Testing Authority for Safety-Sensitive Workers — Seeks Congressional 'Safety Carveout'
items.summary: NDASA (National Drug and Alcohol Screening Association), one of seven designated anti-rescheduling participants in the broader-rescheduling ALJ hearing, testified today July 2, 2026 at DEA headquarters in Arlington, VA. NDASA's pre-filed core argument: moving marijuana from Schedule I to Schedule III would eliminate the federal government's legal authority to drug-test safety-sensitive transportation workers — pilots, truck drivers, air traffic controllers, railroad workers, school bus drivers — for THC. The legal basis: President Reagan's 1986 Executive Order granted HHS authority to certify workplace drug-testing procedures only for Schedule I/II substances; a 1991 federal statute requires all DOT-mandated drug testing to use HHS-certified labs and guidelines. Removing cannabis from Schedule I/II would remove it from HHS's testing jurisdiction and, by extension, eliminate DOT's authority to include THC in mandatory testing panels for safety-sensitive workers. NDASA called former DOT official Patrice Kelly as a supporting expert witness and is urging Congress to pass a statutory 'safety carveout' preserving DOT drug-testing authority independent of the scheduling outcome. Note: actual in-hearing testimony content is unverifiable until DEA releases the official post-hearing transcript (post-July 15); this item reflects NDASA's publicly pre-filed arguments and press statements.
items.whyItMatters: For Oklahoma OMMA-licensed cultivators and dispensaries, DOT drug-testing policy does not directly govern cannabis cultivation or retail operations. However, NDASA's transportation safety argument is one of the most legally distinct claims in the ALJ administrative record — it raises a concrete statutory conflict (Reagan EO + 1991 law) rather than solely a policy or public-health objection. This argument may attract bipartisan Congressional support post-hearing; if Congress passes a 'safety carveout' amendment as a condition of broader Schedule III rescheduling, it establishes a precedent of Congress attaching conditions to cannabis regulatory milestones. Compliance directors should also note that today's July 3 holiday recess means the next testimony day is July 6 (SAM / Kevin Sabet), not tomorrow.

source.name: InOut Labs — NDASA Press Conference on Marijuana Rescheduling and DOT Drug Testing

items.headline: State-Licensed Cannabis Operators Intervene in D.C. Circuit to Defend Rescheduling — First Industry Voices on Government's Side in Cases 26-1106/1130/1136
items.summary: MedPharm Iowa, LLC (d/b/a Bud & Mary's, Iowa) and Tri-Mountain Pure, LLC (Pennsylvania) filed a motion on June 29, 2026 seeking leave to intervene as respondents on the government's side in consolidated D.C. Circuit rescheduling challenges (Cases 26-1106/1130/1136). Both companies had already applied for DEA registrations under the expedited pathway. Their motion argues the federal government could not 'adequately represent' their interests and that they would suffer at least five distinct harms if the litigation blocks rescheduling — including loss of 280E tax relief, loss of federal registration benefits, and operational and competitive harm. The filing was made the same day the broader-rescheduling ALJ hearing opened at DEA headquarters in Arlington, VA; it was first reported in Marijuana Moment's July 2, 2026 newsletter.
items.whyItMatters: This is the first time state-licensed cannabis operators have entered the D.C. Circuit consolidated challenges as active legal defenders of the April 28 order — joining DOJ on the respondent side. Oklahoma cultivators and dispensaries who filed DEA registrations under the expedited window have the same economic and regulatory stake as these Iowa and Pennsylvania operators: loss of 280E relief, loss of safe-harbor registration status, and operational disruption if the order is stayed or vacated. The D.C. Circuit must decide whether to grant the motion; if granted, it would add harm evidence and commercial-impact arguments to the legal defense beyond what DOJ alone presents. The April 28 order remains in effect — no stay has been granted as of July 4, 2026. The intervention filing and the DOJ's strong July 2 response brief (arguing challengers 'come nowhere near' the stay standard) together signal the legal defense of the order is broadening on multiple fronts.

items.headline: ALJ Hearing Day 5 (July 6): SAM/Sabet Testified as Scheduled; Day 6 (July 7): DUID Victim Voices Today — Week 1 Cross-Exam Established FDA Didn't Interview Cannabis-Psychosis or Crash Families
items.summary: Day 5 (July 6, 2026): Kevin Sabet (CEO, Smart Approaches to Marijuana) testified as a designated ALJ hearing participant per Chief ALJ Julius's June 24 published schedule. Formal testimony content is under official blackout until DEA releases the post-hearing transcript after July 15. MMJ International Holdings issued a Newswire press release on July 6 headlined 'Harvard Expert Testifies State Dispensary Cannabis Does Not Meet Pharmaceutical Standards' — MMJ International is a D.C. Circuit litigation petitioner (adverse party to the April 28 order); this release is interested-party advocacy, not verified journalism; the claim does not constitute a regulatory finding. Day 6 (July 7, TODAY): DUID Victim Voices is testifying per the published schedule. WEEK 1 CROSS-EXAMINATION DETAIL (confirmed by Cannabis Business Times): DUID Victim Voices' attorney Patrick Kenneally (Burke Law Group) cross-examined FDA's Dominic Chiapperino during Week 1, establishing on cross that FDA did not interview parents whose children suffered cannabis-related psychosis — nor family members of car accident victims where the driver may have been cannabis-impaired — during FDA's 2023 CAMU scientific and medical evaluation. This is a documented procedural gap in the government's evidentiary record that goes to the completeness of the CAMU analysis.
items.whyItMatters: The Week 1 cross-examination detail — FDA's CAMU analysis excluded cannabis-psychosis families and traffic-accident families as data sources — adds a second evidentiary attack to the Day 2 CAMU five-to-two-part methodology challenge. Together, the opponents are building an administrative record that argues the CAMU finding underlying Schedule III classification was both procedurally incomplete (wrong methodology, missed data sources) and substantively flawed. For Oklahoma operators, the hearing scope remains ONLY adult-use/recreational marijuana per Chief ALJ Julius's June 19 order; state-licensed medical marijuana's Schedule III status is not at issue. MMJ International's Newswire 'Harvard Expert' release is being widely syndicated to look like neutral journalism — it is not; see discourse item watch-mmj-intl-newswire-as-neutral-hearing-journalism. Hearing blackout continues through July 15; the official DEA.gov transcript is the only authoritative record.

items.headline: White House Asks Congress to Delay or Revise November 2026 Hemp THC Ban — House Farm Bill Passed Without Delay; Hemp Planting Predictability Act Pending
items.summary: OMB Director Russell Vought sent a letter to House Speaker Mike Johnson on June 24, 2026 formally requesting that Congress either revise the hemp definition in Section 781 of P.L. 119-37 or delay the November 12, 2026 effective date that will ban hemp-derived products exceeding 0.3% total THC. The House Farm Bill (H.R. 8646) passed 224–200 on April 30, 2026 WITHOUT including a hemp ban delay or definition revision. The Hemp Planting Predictability Act (S.3686 / H.R.7024) — which would extend the implementation deadline to November 12, 2028 — has bipartisan sponsors in both chambers (Senate: Klobuchar D-MN, Paul R-KY, Merkley D-OR; House: Rep. Baird R-IN) but has received no committee hearing as of July 2026. The White House OMB request is a legislative communication, not a regulatory action, enforcement moratorium, or amendment to P.L. 119-37.
items.whyItMatters: Oklahoma dispensaries and hemp operators: Plan for November 12, 2026 compliance. The White House OMB letter signals executive-branch concern about the hemp ban's impact but does not delay or suspend the effective date of Section 781. The House already declined to include a delay in the Farm Bill. Standalone delay legislation exists (Hemp Planting Predictability Act) but has no committee hearing. A delay is politically possible — bipartisan sponsors, White House support — but not assured. The Senate Farm Bill markup is the most likely vehicle; monitor Senate Agriculture Committee activity. Until Congress acts or a federal court enjoins the provision, the November 12, 2026 effective date stands. Oklahoma hemp operators should begin reformulation, product transition, and inventory planning now.

source.name: Cannabis Business Times — White House Asks Congress to Revise Hemp Definition, Delay THC Product Ban

secondarySource.name: Marijuana Moment — House Passes Farm Bill Without Hemp Ban Delay

items.summary: Kenneth Finn MD (Colorado-based pain management physician, co-vice president of the International Academy on the Science and Impacts of Cannabis) testified on Day 7 (July 8, 2026) of the broader-rescheduling ALJ hearing at DEA headquarters in Arlington, VA. AP wire reporting from inside the hearing room — verified journalism from a reporter physically present — confirms Finn called state medical marijuana 'somewhat of a ruse' and testified that 18 of 27 randomized controlled trials examining cannabis for pain showed no benefit. Finn is a designated anti-rescheduling hearing participant — he is testifying as an advocate, not a neutral scientific referee. The official DEA.gov transcript (post-July 15 only) is the authoritative record; AP wire captures in-room journalist observation.
items.whyItMatters: AP wire reporting carries more weight than unverified social media claims, but is not the official DEA transcript. The 'ruse' characterization is now circulating as proof DEA will revoke Schedule III registrations issued to Oklahoma and other state-licensed operators — this interpretation is incorrect. Chief ALJ Julius's June 19 scope order explicitly confirmed the hearing addresses ONLY adult-use/recreational marijuana; Oklahoma state-licensed medical marijuana's Schedule III status is not at issue. items.headline: ALJ Hearing Day 5 (July 6): SAM's Harvard Expert Dr. Madras Acknowledged Under DEA Cross That Marijuana Meets Schedule III Criteria — Marijuana Herald In-Person Report
items.summary: The Marijuana Herald, whose correspondent was physically present at the broader-rescheduling ALJ hearing, reported that Dr. Bertha K. Madras — a Harvard Medical School neuroscientist appearing on behalf of Smart Approaches to Marijuana (SAM) — acknowledged under cross-examination by DEA government counsel that marijuana meets Schedule III criteria. This concession is notable because Madras testified as an opposition witness; under adversarial cross-examination by the government, she acknowledged the scheduling criteria she was appearing to challenge. The official DEA.gov transcript, to be released after July 15, is the authoritative record; this item captures in-person journalist reporting of the cross-examination exchange.
items.whyItMatters: An opposition expert acknowledging under government cross-examination that a key scheduling criterion is met is a significant moment in the administrative record. items.headline: American Bankers Association Formally Endorses SAFER Banking Act — Sends Letter to Congress Urging Passage (July 7, 2026)
items.summary: The American Bankers Association (ABA) sent a formal letter to Congressional leadership on July 7, 2026 urging passage of the SAFER Banking Act (S.4942 / H.R.9471), which would bar federal regulators from penalizing financial institutions for serving state-legal cannabis businesses and explicitly extends safe harbor to ancillary businesses including landlords, technology vendors, accounting firms, and security providers. ABA is the primary trade association for the U.S. banking industry, representing banks of all sizes. The SAFER Banking Act was reintroduced with bipartisan support on June 25, 2026 (Senate: Merkley D-OR, Murkowski R-AK, Warren D-MA, Daines R-MT; House: Himes D-CT, Davidson R-OH, and others).
items.whyItMatters: ABA's institutional endorsement adds establishment banking-industry credibility to the SAFER Banking push. Oklahoma dispensaries and cultivators continue to face banking access challenges — accounts closed without notice, cash-only operations, limited payment processing. ABA's letter does not enact the law; Senate passage remains the key obstacle (the bill has cleared the House multiple times). However, formal ABA backing increases the bill's political viability in a Congress already engaged on cannabis rescheduling. Oklahoma operators should not change banking arrangements based on an endorsement letter alone.

source.name: American Bankers Association — SAFER Banking Act Endorsement Letter (July 7, 2026)

items.headline: Senate Agriculture Committee Farm Bill Markup Window July 13 – ~August 7, 2026 — Last 2026 Vehicle for Hemp Ban Delay; Senate Draft Does Not Include Delay
items.summary: The Senate Agriculture Committee's markup window for the 2026 Farm Bill runs July 13 through approximately August 7, 2026 — the last viable 2026 legislative vehicle for inserting a delay or revision to the November 12, 2026 federal hemp THC ban (Section 781 of P.L. 119-37). The House Farm Bill (H.R. 8646) passed April 30, 2026 WITHOUT a hemp ban delay. The Senate draft circulating as of July 2026 reportedly does not include a hemp ban delay. The White House OMB Director Vought formally requested Congress delay or revise the hemp definition in a June 24 letter to Speaker Johnson — executive-branch support for delay exists but has not translated to legislative text. The standalone Hemp Planting Predictability Act (S.3686) has bipartisan sponsors but has received no committee hearing and remains dormant.
items.whyItMatters: The Senate markup window is the last realistic opportunity in 2026 to attach a hemp ban delay to moving legislation. With both the House-passed Farm Bill and the Senate draft reportedly excluding a delay, the probability of legislative relief before November 12 is decreasing. Oklahoma operators selling hemp-derived Delta-8, Delta-10, THCA-flower, or other products exceeding 0.3% total THC (including THCA) must plan for November 12, 2026 compliance now — treat any Senate markup delay as a windfall, not a planning assumption.

items.headline: ALJ Hearing Day 8 (July 10): TBI's Erica Stephens Argues Rescheduling Proposal is 'Deficient' — Eight-Factor Analysis 'Overlooks or Ignores Substantial Evidence'; Hearing Recesses Through July 12
items.summary: Erica Stephens, assistant special agent in charge of the Tennessee Dangerous Drugs Task Force, testified for the Tennessee Bureau of Investigation (TBI) on Day 8 (July 10, 2026) of the broader-rescheduling ALJ hearing at DEA headquarters in Arlington, VA. Stephens argued the government's marijuana rescheduling proposal is 'deficient' and 'overlooks or ignores substantial evidence relevant to the required eight-factor analysis that weighs strongly against rescheduling marijuana to Schedule III.' Her testimony focused on how deregulation of marijuana — including the legalization of hemp — makes TBI's law enforcement mission more difficult, encourages involvement of criminal organizations in marijuana production and distribution, and negatively impacts the health and safety of Tennesseans. The hearing is now on weekend recess through July 12; it reconvenes Monday July 13 with Phillip A. Drum PharmD, then the state coalition (Nebraska, Idaho, Indiana) on July 14, before concluding July 15. Official DEA.gov transcript releases post-July 15 only — no verified detailed testimony content available until then.
items.whyItMatters: TBI's eight-factor adequacy challenge is the most procedurally direct attack on the rescheduling methodology entered into the administrative record so far: the argument is not simply that marijuana is harmful (a policy position) but that the government's formal CSA eight-factor scheduling analysis omitted or ignored substantial evidence — an administrative record challenge that, if credited by the ALJ, could form the basis for a recommended denial of Schedule III classification for adult-use marijuana. This is structurally similar to Day 2's CAMU methodology attack. For Oklahoma OMMA-licensed operators, the hearing scope is exclusively adult-use/recreational marijuana per Chief ALJ Julius's June 19 scope order; state-licensed medical marijuana's Schedule III status, OBNDD registration obligations, and 280E relief are not subject to the hearing and remain in effect. The remaining hearing schedule (Drum PharmD July 13, States July 14, conclusion July 15) will complete the opposition's evidentiary record; the ALJ's recommended decision on adult-use rescheduling will follow on an indeterminate timeline — historically, ALJ recommended decisions have taken two or more years. No stay of the April 28 order has been granted as of July 12, 2026.

items.headline: Senate Agriculture Committee Farm Bill Markup Begins July 13 — Chair Boozman Pre-Excludes Hemp Delay Amendments; November 12 Ban Now Near-Certain to Take Effect
items.summary: The Senate Agriculture Committee's Farm Bill markup begins Monday July 13, 2026 — the last viable 2026 legislative vehicle for inserting a delay to the November 12, 2026 federal hemp THC ban (Section 781 of P.L. 119-37). Chair John Boozman released the Senate draft on June 23, 2026 without any hemp ban delay language; a Senate Agriculture Committee aide stated that allowing senators to offer amendments on hemp and other controversial issues at the markup 'would preclude us from moving forward' to the Senate floor — Boozman is targeting 60 votes for floor passage and hemp delay provisions would cost him those votes. The House Farm Bill (passed April 30, 2026) also contains no hemp delay. The standalone Hemp Planting Predictability Act (S.3686/H.R.7024) has bipartisan sponsors but has received no committee hearing and remains dormant. The White House OMB Director Vought's June 24 letter requesting a delay has not produced legislative text.
items.whyItMatters: Oklahoma dispensaries and hemp operators: the Senate markup window that was described as the 'last viable 2026 legislative vehicle' for hemp delay is now effectively closed before it opens. Boozman's pre-exclusion of hemp amendments means neither the Senate Farm Bill markup nor the House-passed bill provides any delay. Unless a standalone bill (S.3686) moves independently — which has zero committee-hearing history — or a federal court enjoins Section 781, the November 12, 2026 effective date stands. Products exceeding 0.3% total THC (including THCA) or 0.4mg THC per final container will revert to Schedule I on November 12. Oklahoma operators selling hemp-derived Delta-8, THCA-flower, or other affected products must treat November 12 as a hard planning deadline and not defer reformulation or inventory transition.

items.headline: OMMA Permanent Rules Take Effect July 11, 2026 — Physician Registration, Transporter Warehouse Storage, DUI Warning Labels, Employee Credential Updates
items.summary: New OMMA permanent rules took effect July 11, 2026, implementing 2025 legislative session bills HB 2807, SB 518, SB 774, SB 1039, and SB 1066. Key changes: (1) HB 2807 — commercial transporters may use warehouse locations for temporary storage of medical marijuana, provided all storage is documented, tracked, and traceable in Metrc; amends background check and prepackaging requirements; (2) SB 518 — adds DUI warning notices to required medical marijuana product labels; (3) SB 1066 (reminder: effective January 1, 2026) — physicians must complete medical marijuana education and register with OMMA before providing patient recommendations; must remain in good standing with state medical licensing boards; annual continuing education required; (4) Employee credential validity updated to one year from date of issuance; (5) Display samples up to 3.5 grams now permitted; (6) Lab testing rule clarifications. OMMA issued a reminder bulletin accompanying the July 11 rules noting physician recommendation requirement compliance ('Reminder: Physician Medical Marijuana Recommendation Requirements Now in Effect').
items.whyItMatters: CAKE is an Oklahoma OMMA-licensed cultivator and processor — the July 11 permanent rules are now in effect and change operational requirements immediately. Transporter warehouse storage changes affect logistics partners and any transportation arrangements CAKE uses. DUI warning label additions are mandatory for all medical marijuana products as of July 11; packaging and labeling must comply now, not at next product refresh. Employee credential validity updated to one year from issuance — audit current employee credentials for expiration dates and schedule renewals. Display sample allowance (up to 3.5g) may affect dispensary customer display practices for retail partners.

items.headline: Department of War Issues Memo (July 9): Schedule III Rescheduling Does NOT Authorize Service Member Marijuana Use — UCMJ Prohibition Remains in Full Effect
items.summary: The Department of War (DOW) issued a one-page memo dated July 9, 2026, signed by Under Secretary Anthony J. Tata (under secretary of war for personnel and readiness) and obtained by Marijuana Moment. The memo makes clear to senior Pentagon leadership that federal rescheduling of marijuana to Schedule III does not authorize marijuana use by military service members or DOW civilian employees. The DOW 'maintains a drug-free workplace to protect national security, public safety, and operational readiness.' Under the UCMJ, 'Service members are reminded that the Uniform Code of Military Justice (UCMJ) prohibits certain actions, including the wrongful use, possession, manufacture, or distribution of marijuana. These offenses remain punishable under the UCMJ, even if a State or local jurisdiction legalizes the use of recreational marijuana or authorizes the sale of marijuana, including medical marijuana, at State dispensaries.' Service members who violate the UCMJ may face judicial, nonjudicial, or administrative action. The sole exception: FDA-approved drug formulations with a valid prescription for a legitimate medical purpose. The memo notably uses the phrase 'if implemented' when referring to rescheduling, suggesting DOW legal staff have not fully reconciled with the April 28 order's finality.
items.whyItMatters: The DOW memo is a concrete illustration of the siloed nature of federal Schedule III implementation — different agencies are independently deciding the practical effect of the April 28 order without unified federal guidance. For CAKE and its Oklahoma wholesale dispensary customers, the direct compliance impact is limited: cultivator and dispensary employees are not subject to the UCMJ. However, the memo has two broader implications: (1) any employee who is also a National Guard member, military reservist, or DOW civilian employee cannot invoke OMMA licensing or Schedule III status to protect their military career — state medical dispensary authorization is explicitly insufficient under DOW policy; (2) the memo reinforces the NDASA DOT drug-testing 'safety carveout' argument raised at the ALJ hearing (July 2) and keeps the political pressure for agency-by-agency carveouts active in Congress. The 'if implemented' language signals that at least one federal agency's legal staff may be treating the April 28 order as potentially reversible — consistent with active D.C. Circuit litigation risk. Oklahoma operators who also hold federal contractor status, employ military reservists, or operate in defense-adjacent sectors should seek counsel specific to their situation.

source.name: Marijuana Moment — Military Warns That Trump's Marijuana Rescheduling Move Doesn't Allow Use By Service Members

items.headline: ALJ Julius Issues Post-Hearing Scheduling Order July 16 — August 17 Deadline for Optional Briefs and Transcript Corrections; Official Transcript Not Until Late August/September
items.summary: Chief ALJ Derek Julius issued a post-hearing scheduling order on July 16, 2026, following the July 15 conclusion of the broader-rescheduling ALJ hearing. The order sets August 17, 2026 as the deadline for two distinct items: (1) OPTIONAL post-hearing written briefs (≤50 pages, nonmandatory) per 21 C.F.R. § 1316.64 — designated parties may submit written closing arguments but are not required to; and (2) proposed transcript corrections — parties may flag transcription errors (misspellings, wrong speaker attribution) but may NOT revise or alter the substance of testimony. After reviewing proposed corrections, Julius will issue a second order adopting a final correction list; the official DEA.gov hearing transcript will NOT be published until after that second order issues — late August 2026 at the earliest, likely September 2026. After all post-hearing briefs are received by August 17, the ALJ will draft a recommended decision for the DEA Administrator; there is NO statutory deadline for that recommended decision. Harris Sliwoski LLP confirmed this in their July 16 post-hearing analysis ('The Quietest Fight of DEA's Life'): 'there is no statutory deadline for the ALJ to issue a recommended decision after post-hearing briefs are submitted.' After the ALJ recommended decision issues, designated parties have 20 days to file exceptions; the full record then passes to the DEA Administrator for the final decision. The D.C. Circuit stay motion in Cases 26-1106/1130/1136 is procedurally independent; petitioners' reply brief still pending as of July 17, 2026.
items.whyItMatters: August 17, 2026 is a procedural administrative milestone — NOT a decision date and NOT an operator compliance deadline. Oklahoma OMMA-licensed operators should note: (1) August 17 is the deadline for optional post-hearing briefs and transcript correction proposals only — no ALJ ruling or recommendation issues on that date; (2) the official DEA.gov hearing transcript will not be available until late August at the earliest — do not rely on early-circulating 'transcript' documents or unverified summaries; (3) no ALJ recommended decision is expected by August 17 or in the near term — prior DEA marijuana scheduling proceedings took one to three years from hearing close to recommended decision; (4) OBNDD enforcement grace period (January 1, 2027) is unchanged and unrelated to the ALJ post-hearing schedule; (5) the August 17 brief deadline is also distinct from and has no bearing on the D.C. Circuit's timeline for ruling on the stay motion in Cases 26-1106/1130/1136. Harris Sliwoski LLP's post-hearing analysis confirmed the ALJ has no statutory clock forcing a recommended decision after receiving briefs — August 17 starts a multi-year process, not a weeks-long one.

source.name: Marijuana Herald — DEA Judge Sets August 17 Deadline for Final Briefs (July 16, 2026)

secondarySource.name: Harris Sliwoski LLP — The Quietest Fight of DEA's Life: Reflections on the Marijuana Rescheduling Hearing (July 16, 2026)

items.headline: Senate Democrats Reintroduce CAOA — Full Cannabis Descheduling Bill; No Republican Cosponsors, No Path to Passage in Current Senate
items.summary: Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer (D-NY), Sen. Cory Booker (D-NJ), and Sen. Ron Wyden (D-OR), joined by 15 additional Democratic cosponsors, reintroduced the Cannabis Administration and Opportunity Act (CAOA) on July 16, 2026. The bill would fully remove cannabis from the Controlled Substances Act — going further than the April 28 rescheduling to Schedule III — and establish an FDA Center for Cannabis Products to regulate production, labeling, distribution, and retail. Additional provisions: expunge certain federal cannabis convictions; restore federal benefits lost over past cannabis convictions; protect noncitizens from deportation tied to cannabis activity; direct tax revenue to an Opportunity Trust Fund for communities harmed by prohibition; extend worker protections to the cannabis industry. The 2026 CAOA also addresses hemp by imposing THC limits on hemp-derived products and classifying products exceeding those limits as marijuana. This is the third formal CAOA introduction since 2022; neither prior version advanced beyond committee referral. No Republican cosponsors joined the filing.
items.whyItMatters: The CAOA reintroduction has no path to passage in the current Republican-controlled Senate — it is a legislative signal and political positioning document, not imminent law. For Oklahoma OMMA-licensed cultivators and dispensaries, nothing in the CAOA affects current Schedule III status, OBNDD registration obligations, 280E treatment, or the D.C. Circuit litigation. The bill's hemp THC limits provision would, if enacted, affect Oklahoma hemp and delta-8 operators already bracing for the November 12, 2026 hemp redefinition. The more immediate risk for Oklahoma operators remains the D.C. Circuit stay motion (reply brief pending) and the OBNDD January 1, 2027 enforcement grace period — the CAOA does not change either timeline.

source.name: NORML — Senate Democrats Reintroduce Comprehensive Cannabis Descheduling Legislation (July 16, 2026)

secondarySource.name: Forbes — Democratic Senators Reintroduce Federal Marijuana Legalization Bill (July 16, 2026)

items.headline: SAM and NDASA File Brief Opposing Cannabis Companies' Intervention in D.C. Circuit — Argue MedPharm Iowa and Tri-Mountain 'Have Not Identified Any New Arguments'
items.summary: Anti-rescheduling petitioners Smart Approaches to Marijuana (SAM) and the National Drug and Alcohol Screening Association (NDASA) filed a brief in the D.C. Circuit opposing the motion of MedPharm Iowa, LLC (d/b/a Bud & Mary's, Iowa) and Tri-Mountain Pure, LLC (Pennsylvania) to intervene as respondents on the government's side in consolidated Cases 26-1106/1130/1136. SAM and NDASA argued the cannabis companies 'have not identified any argument they would make that would not be pressed by the government' and therefore are 'not entitled to intervention.' The cannabis companies had filed their intervention motion June 29, 2026 — the same day the ALJ hearing opened — arguing the government alone could not 'adequately represent' their interests; both companies had already applied for DEA Schedule III registration under the expedited pathway. The D.C. Circuit must decide whether to grant or deny the intervention motion before MedPharm Iowa and Tri-Mountain become formal parties in the consolidated challenges. No court ruling on the intervention motion has been issued as of July 17, 2026. The petitioners' reply brief on the stay motion also remains pending as of July 17, 2026.
items.whyItMatters: SAM and NDASA's opposition to the cannabis companies' intervention means the D.C. Circuit must rule on the procedural motion before industry voices formally join the litigation defense. A denied intervention would leave the government (DOJ) as the sole formal defender of the April 28 order in the D.C. Circuit — though the intervenors could still file amicus briefs if intervention is denied. For Oklahoma OMMA operators who registered under the expedited window (like MedPharm Iowa and Tri-Mountain Pure), the outcome of the intervention motion has no direct effect on their DEA registration or OBNDD compliance obligations. However, intervention denial would weaken the breadth of arguments available in the D.C. Circuit defense of the April 28 order, slightly increasing litigation risk. The stay motion reply brief remains the more consequential pending event — a granted stay could freeze the April 28 order regardless of who is a formal party.

items.headline: OMMA Enforcement Surge in 2026 — 300+ Cases Filed, Emergency Suspensions for Minor Violations; Industry Charges 'Going Overboard'
items.summary: FOX23 and KTUL reported July 18, 2026 that Oklahoma's medical marijuana industry is pushing back against what operators describe as aggressive OMMA enforcement — including emergency summary suspension orders for violations characterized as minor and correctable under state statute. A business owner reported a sudden shutdown on approximately July 6, 2026 over missing inventory tags, described as a correctable violation; OMMA issued an emergency summary suspension order despite six years of successful inspections. An industry attorney reported more than 300 enforcement cases filed by OMMA in 2026 to date. In a bulletin titled 'Continued Progress: Recent Administrative Actions,' OMMA reported 17 administrative actions in the past three weeks, including embargo of thousands of unregulated medical marijuana plants and products. OMMA separately issued emergency orders of summary suspension and embargo against multiple licensees in 2026, including AAA Cannabis Distribution LLC (inventory discrepancies — nearly 3,000 infused pre-rolls and approximately 5 lbs of flower and concentrate with unreconcilable Metrc records), Temple of Kush LLC, Ohana Canna LLC, ReLeaf Medical Dispensary LLC, and Jinshan LLC. OMMA stated enforcement follows state administrative processes and that all regulatory violations are taken seriously to protect patient safety.
items.whyItMatters: For CAKE as an Oklahoma OMMA-licensed cultivator and processor, OMMA's 2026 enforcement surge signals an elevated inspection and enforcement environment. Metrc accuracy is the primary enforcement trigger — OMMA routinely compares physical inventory against digital Metrc records, and discrepancies are resulting in emergency summary suspensions. CAKE and its wholesale dispensary customers should audit Metrc records against physical inventory immediately and correct any discrepancies before routine inspections occur. Emergency summary suspension authority allows OMMA to shut a business with no advance notice; the July 6 incident (emergency suspension over missing tags) illustrates that even correctable, non-egregious violations are being treated as emergency enforcement matters. Industry attorney characterization of enforcement as 'going overboard' reflects broader political tension — Governor Stitt has publicly stated he wants to eliminate the medical marijuana program, and enforcement-heavy posture may be aligned with that pressure. The OBNDD January 1, 2027 grace period for DEA registration does not suspend OMMA's existing enforcement authority.

source.name: FOX23 — Is the OMMA Going Overboard With Punitive Measures? (July 18, 2026)

items.headline: Supreme Court Rules 9-0: Federal Drug-User Gun Ban Cannot Apply to Casual Marijuana Users — United States v. Hemani (June 18, 2026)
items.summary: The U.S. Supreme Court unanimously ruled 9-0 on June 18, 2026 in United States v. Hemani (No. 24-1234) that 18 U.S.C. § 922(g)(3) — the federal law criminalizing firearm possession by an 'unlawful user of or addicted to any controlled substance,' punishable by up to 15 years — violates the Second Amendment as applied to casual marijuana users. Justice Gorsuch wrote for the unanimous Court. The ruling is narrow: the government must demonstrate that a defendant used marijuana regularly to the point of incapacity before a § 922(g)(3) prosecution is constitutionally permissible. The case arose from Ali Hemani, a Texas resident who told FBI agents he used marijuana approximately every other day while legally owning firearms. The Court analogized § 922(g)(3) to historical statutes that disarmed only 'habitual drunkards,' rejecting the argument that any drug use is a sufficient basis to strip Second Amendment rights. The ruling does not legalize marijuana, does not change federal Schedule I or III status for any marijuana category, and does not provide a basis to avoid OBNDD or OMMA compliance obligations.
items.whyItMatters: Oklahoma OMMA-licensed operators, dispensary employees, and registered patients who are also legal firearms owners have significantly greater Second Amendment protection after Hemani. An employee who uses medical marijuana occasionally cannot be federally prosecuted under § 922(g)(3) solely for owning a firearm — the government must first demonstrate habitual, incapacitating use. Practical implications: dispensaries and cultivation facilities that had policies restricting employee firearm ownership premised on § 922(g)(3) exposure should reassess those policies with counsel. The ruling does NOT affect OMMA licensing, OBNDD registration obligations, or 280E tax treatment — compliance requirements are entirely unchanged. The ruling is narrow: heavy or habitual users may still face prosecution. Employees who are also National Guard or reserve service members are governed by UCMJ independently of this ruling (see DoW memo, July 9, 2026). Operators with federal-contractor employees should separately evaluate security-clearance implications.

source.name: Supreme Court — United States v. Hemani, No. 24-1234 (June 18, 2026)

secondarySource.name: SCOTUSblog — Court sides with challenger to law banning drug users from possessing guns

items.headline: D.C. Circuit Stay Motion FULLY BRIEFED as of July 17 — NDASA and MMJ International Filed Reply; Court Can Rule at Any Time
items.summary: NDASA and MMJ International Holdings filed their reply brief on or around July 17, 2026 in consolidated D.C. Circuit Cases 26-1106/1130/1136, completing the three-round stay-motion briefing cycle: (1) NDASA/MMJ stay motion filed June 9; (2) DOJ opposition filed July 2 (challengers 'come nowhere near' the stay standard; NDASA lacks standing; interests are 'pocketbook' concerns); (3) petitioners' reply brief filed July 17. The stay motion is now fully before the D.C. Circuit and the court can issue a ruling at any time — there is no statutory deadline forcing a quick decision. Reply brief arguments (per Newswire/HealthTechnologyNet reporting): (1) Binding D.C. Circuit precedent in NORML v. DEA (1977) required notice-and-comment rulemaking under 21 U.S.C. § 811(d)(1) before rescheduling marijuana via treaty-based authority — the April 28, 2026 Final Order bypassed that process; (2) irreparable harm is ongoing as state-licensed operators consolidate federally sanctioned market share while challengers' investment is stripped of competitive distinction; (3) DOJ's 'pocketbook interests' standing characterization is disputed. No oral argument has been scheduled; the D.C. Circuit frequently rules on stay motions on the papers. The rescheduling order remains in effect unless and until the court grants a stay. MedPharm Iowa / Tri-Mountain Pure intervention motion (seeking to join the DOJ's defense) also remains pending without a ruling.
items.whyItMatters: A D.C. Circuit stay of the April 28 order would freeze Schedule III status and potentially unwind DEA registrations filed under it — the single highest legal risk to Oklahoma operators' 280E relief, DEA registration investments, and operational planning. Now that the stay motion is fully briefed, a D.C. Circuit ruling is the next critical legal milestone. Oklahoma operators should: (1) not assume a stay denial is certain — the court has not spoken and standing arguments cut both ways; (2) not halt compliance preparations pending a ruling — OBNDD's January 1, 2027 enforcement grace period is unchanged; (3) monitor pacer.gov or cannabis law firm alert services for a ruling when it issues. The Nebraska/Indiana state petitioners' merits briefing on the broader APA challenge is also proceeding on a separate track.
